platform.title: Practice Case: Right Lower Quadrant Pain

vignette:
A 38-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "Fatigue and swelling for 2 months."

Vital Signs
BP 148/94
HR 78
RR 16
Temp 98.4°F
SpO₂ 98%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: CKD Stage 4 is the most likely diagnosis. GFR 22 - must find cause.
reasoning: Attributing to diabetes (he has none)
Not asking about occupation and heat exposure
Missing that HTN is secondary, not cause

illnessScript.epidemiology: Chronic kidney disease of unknown etiology (CKDu) in agricultural communities: Sri Lanka, Central America, India. Affects young male agricultural workers. Not explained by traditional CKD risk factors.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Etiology unclear: heat stress/dehydration, nephrotoxic agrochemicals, heavy metals, infections proposed. Chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis pattern. Slowly progressive to ESRD.
illnessScript.workup: Elevated creatinine, reduced eGFR in absence of diabetes, hypertension, glomerulonephritis. Bland urinary sediment (minimal proteinuria). Renal biopsy shows tubulointerstitial nephritis.
illnessScript.treatment: No specific treatment. Supportive CKD management. Hydration and heat stress prevention. Avoid NSAIDs, nephrotoxins. Dialysis or transplant for ESRD. Prevention: occupational health measures.

distractors.explanation: Young, no DM, no HTN, minimal proteinuria, agricultural work.
No diabetes.

platform.title: Practice Case: Recurrent Swelling Without Hives

vignette:
A 24-year-old female presents to the clinic, stating: "Burning with urination for 2 days."

Vital Signs
BP 118/74
HR 72
RR 14
Temp 98.8°F
SpO₂ 99%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Vaginitis is the most likely diagnosis. Can cause external dysuria - ask about discharge, odor.
reasoning: Not distinguishing internal vs external dysuria (UTI vs vaginitis)
Missing STI - always ask about sexual history
Treating without culture in complicated patients
Forgetting pregnancy test in reproductive-age women

evidence.title: IDSA/ESCMID Guidelines: Acute Uncomplicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis 2010
evidence.cite: Gupta K, et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2011;52(5):e103-e120
points: CYSTITIS FIRST-LINE: Nitrofurantoin 100mg BID x 5 days (A-I); TMP-SMX DS BID x 3 days if resistance <20% (A-I); Fosfomycin 3g single dose (A-I)
AVOID fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated cystitis - reserve for pyelonephritis/complicated UTI due to collateral damage (A-III)
Beta-lactams (amoxicillin-clavulanate, cephalosporins) are ALTERNATIVES only - inferior efficacy vs other agents (B-I)
Amoxicillin/ampicillin should NOT be used for empiric treatment - high resistance rates (A-III)

illnessScript.epidemiology: 50-60% of women will have UTI in lifetime. Risk factors: female sex, sexual activity, spermicide use, prior UTI, post-menopause, diabetes, urinary catheter. E. coli causes 75-95% of uncomplicated cases.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Uropathogenic bacteria ascend from periurethral area → bladder colonization → epithelial invasion → inflammatory response → mucosal edema and irritation → dysuria, frequency, urgency. Complicated UTI involves structural/functional abnormality or resistant organisms.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 118/74, HR 72, Temp 98.8°F - afebrile with normal vitals.; Well-appearing young woman, no acute distress.; Soft. Mild suprapubic tenderness. No rebound. No CVA tenderness.
illnessScript.workup: Uncomplicated: clinical diagnosis sufficient (dysuria + frequency without vaginal symptoms). Urinalysis if uncertain. Urine culture for complicated UTI, recurrent UTI, or treatment failure.
illnessScript.treatment: Uncomplicated cystitis: nitrofurantoin 100mg BID x5 days (first-line) OR TMP-SMX x3 days (if local resistance <20%) OR fosfomycin single dose. Avoid fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated UTI.

distractors.explanation: Must assess for fever, flank pain to rule out upper tract infection.
Sexually active - must consider chlamydia/gonorrhea.
Chronic symptoms, negative cultures - not this acute presentation.

platform.title: Practice Case: Increased Thirst and Urination

vignette:
A 72-year-old male presents to the emergency department, stating: "I can't urinate and my belly hurts terribly."

Vital Signs
BP 168/94
HR 92
RR 18
Temp 98.4°F
SpO₂ 97%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Acute Urinary Retention (BPH) is the most likely diagnosis. Elderly male with longstanding LUTS, enlarged prostate, unable to void. Classic BPH-related retention.
reasoning: Delaying catheterization while waiting for workup
Not monitoring for post-obstructive diuresis after catheter placement
Removing catheter too soon without alpha-blocker trial
Missing medication causes (antihistamines, decongestants, anticholinergics)

evidence.title: AUA/SUFU Guideline on the Management of BPH 2021
evidence.cite: Lerner LB, et al. J Urol. 2021;206(4):806-826
points: Acute urinary retention requires immediate catheterization
Post-obstructive diuresis monitoring after catheter placement
Alpha-blocker trial before catheter removal increases success
Trial without catheter (TWOC) at 2-3 days with alpha-blocker

illnessScript.epidemiology: 50-60% of women will have UTI in lifetime. Risk factors: female sex, sexual activity, spermicide use, prior UTI, post-menopause, diabetes, urinary catheter. E. coli causes 75-95% of uncomplicated cases.

illnessScript.workup: Uncomplicated: clinical diagnosis sufficient (dysuria + frequency without vaginal symptoms). Urinalysis if uncertain. Urine culture for complicated UTI, recurrent UTI, or treatment failure.
illnessScript.treatment: Uncomplicated cystitis: nitrofurantoin 100mg BID x5 days (first-line) OR TMP-SMX x3 days (if local resistance <20%) OR fosfomycin single dose. Avoid fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated UTI.

distractors.explanation: Prolonged retention can cause bilateral hydronephrosis and renal failure. Check creatinine.
Can present similarly to BPH. Need PSA and possible biopsy after acute episode resolves.
Can precipitate retention but not primary cause. Usually has dysuria, frequency, urgency before retention.
Can cause intermittent obstruction. Usually has positional symptoms, hematuria.

options.text: Sign of renal failure - urgent nephrology consult

vignette:
A 34-year-old male presents to the emergency department, stating: "Worst pain of my life in my side that goes to my groin."

Vital Signs
BP 158/96
HR 102
RR 18
Temp 99.1°F
SpO₂ 98%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Ureteral Stone (Nephrolithiasis) is the most likely diagnosis. Classic presentation: sudden colicky flank pain radiating to groin, microscopic hematuria, young male.
reasoning: Not ruling out AAA in older patients with flank pain
Missing infected obstructed kidney (fever + stone = emergency)
Over-imaging with CT in young patients with classic presentation and hematuria
Not providing adequate analgesia - renal colic is extremely painful

evidence.title: AUA/Endourology Society Guideline on Kidney Stones 2014 (Amended 2021)
evidence.cite: Assimos D, et al. J Urol. 2016;196(4):1153-1160
points: CT without contrast is gold standard for diagnosis
Stones <10mm: trial of passage with MET (medical expulsive therapy) for 4-6 weeks
Stone >10mm or failed passage: urologic intervention (URS, SWL, or PCNL)
Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin) facilitate stone passage for distal ureteral stones

illnessScript.epidemiology: Lifetime prevalence 10-15%. Peak age 20-50, M>F. 80% calcium stones (oxalate > phosphate), 10% uric acid, 10% struvite/cystine. Recurrence 50% within 5-10 years. Risk: low fluid intake, high sodium/protein diet, obesity.

illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 158/96 (elevated from pain), HR 102, Temp 99.1°F (afebrile), SpO2 98%; Young man writhing in pain, cannot find comfortable position. Pacing the room. Diaphoretic.; Soft, non-tender to palpation. No guarding or rebound. Bowel sounds present.
illnessScript.workup: Non-contrast CT (gold standard, 95% sensitivity). Stone analysis if passed/retrieved. 24-hour urine collection for recurrent stone formers. BMP (calcium, uric acid), urinalysis.
illnessScript.treatment: Acute: analgesia (NSAIDs first-line), hydration, alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) for MET if <10mm. Urology referral if >10mm, infection, obstruction, or intractable pain. Prevention: fluid intake >2.5L/day, dietary modifications based on stone type.

distractors.explanation: Stone + fever + pyuria = EMERGENCY. Requires emergent decompression.
Always consider in patients >50 with flank pain, especially with vascular risk factors.
Would be constant, reproduced with movement/palpation. Not colicky.
Right flank pain can mimic appendicitis. CT will differentiate.

vignette:

Vital Signs
BP 98/62
HR 96
RR 18
Temp 98.4°F
SpO₂ 96% on room air
stem: This 68-year-old man with CKD Stage 3 (baseline Cr 1.6) on lisinopril presents with oliguria, fatigue, and confusion after 10 days of high-dose ibuprofen for gout. He has had decreased oral intake with vomiting. Labs show Cr 5.2, BUN 78, K 5.8. What is the most likely mechanism of his AKI?

options.text: Acute interstitial nephritis from ibuprofen allergy
Prerenal azotemia from volume depletion alone

teaching.keyPoint: Correct! This is the classic 'triple whammy': 1) ACE inhibitor (blocks angiotensin-mediated efferent arteriolar constriction), 2) NSAID (blocks prostaglandin-mediated afferent arteriolar dilation), 3) Volume depletion (reduced renal perfusion). Combined, these severely reduce GFR in vulnerable CKD kidneys.
reasoning: While he has BPH, there's no palpable bladder and he has multiple risk factors for prerenal/intrinsic AKI. Obstruction doesn't explain the timeline with NSAID use.
Volume depletion is a major contributor, but pure prerenal AKI doesn't fully explain the severity in the context of NSAIDs + ACE inhibitor. This is likely a combination.
AIN is possible with NSAIDs but typically presents with fever, rash, and eosinophilia. He lacks these features. The hemodynamic mechanism is more likely given the clinical picture.

evidence.title: KDIGO AKI Guidelines 2012
evidence.cite: Kidney Int Suppl 2012;2:1-138
points: AKI staged by creatinine rise or urine output decrease
Stage 1: Cr 1.5-1.9x baseline or <0.5 mL/kg/hr x 6-12h
Stage 2: Cr 2.0-2.9x baseline or <0.5 mL/kg/hr x ≥12h

illnessScript.epidemiology: Occurs in 5-7% of hospitalized patients, 50% of ICU patients. Causes: prerenal (hypovolemia, heart failure, sepsis) 40-55%, intrinsic (ATN, AIN, glomerulonephritis) 35-50%, postrenal (obstruction) 5-10%. Mortality increases 5-10x with AKI.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Prerenal: decreased renal perfusion → reversible if perfusion restored. ATN: tubular injury from ischemia or nephrotoxins → tubular cell death, cast formation, backleak. Recovery depends on tubular regeneration. AKI increases risk of CKD progression.
illnessScript.workup: Creatinine trend (KDIGO staging), urinalysis (casts, protein), urine electrolytes (FENa <1% suggests prerenal, >2% suggests ATN). Renal ultrasound if obstruction suspected. Avoid contrast; adjust medications.
illnessScript.treatment: Prerenal: volume resuscitation, treat underlying cause. ATN: supportive care, avoid nephrotoxins. Postrenal: relieve obstruction (Foley, nephrostomy). RRT if: refractory hyperkalemia, acidosis, volume overload, uremic symptoms, certain intoxications.

distractors.explanation: While he has BPH, there's no palpable bladder and he has multiple risk factors for prerenal/intrinsic AKI. Obstruction doesn't explain the timeline with NSAID use.

distractors.text: Prerenal azotemia from volume depletion alone
distractors.explanation: Volume depletion is a major contributor, but pure prerenal AKI doesn't fully explain the severity in the context of NSAIDs + ACE inhibitor. This is likely a combination.

distractors.text: Acute interstitial nephritis from ibuprofen allergy
distractors.explanation: AIN is possible with NSAIDs but typically presents with fever, rash, and eosinophilia. He lacks these features. The hemodynamic mechanism is more likely given the clinical picture.

platform.title: Practice Case: Fatigue and Feeling Weak

vignette:
A 68-year-old male presents to the emergency department, stating: "I haven't been feeling right for a few days - really tired and I've noticed I'm not urinating as much."

Vital Signs
BP 98/62
HR 96
RR 18
Temp 98.4°F
SpO₂ 96% on room air
stem: Initial management includes stopping NSAIDs and ACE inhibitor, IV fluid resuscitation. After 2L NS, his BP improves to 118/72. Repeat labs show Cr 4.8, K 5.4. Urine output is 40 mL/hr. What is the next priority?

options.text: Start IV steroids for presumed acute interstitial nephritis
Continue IV fluids, monitor closely, address hyperkalemia, hold nephrotoxins
Restart lisinopril at lower dose to protect kidneys

teaching.keyPoint: Correct! He's improving with fluids (Cr trending down, urine output recovering). Continue supportive care: IV fluids titrated to euvolemia, treat hyperkalemia (calcium if ECG changes, kayexalate, insulin/glucose), hold all nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, ACE inhibitor), hold metformin. Monitor for need for dialysis but not indicated yet.
reasoning: He's not currently uremic enough to require emergent dialysis - no refractory hyperkalemia, no severe acidosis, no uremic pericarditis/encephalopathy. He's responding to fluids.
ACE inhibitors should be held during acute kidney injury! They reduce GFR and can worsen AKI. May restart cautiously once creatinine stabilizes closer to baseline.
There's no strong evidence of AIN (no fever, rash, eosinophilia). The hemodynamic mechanism is more likely. Steroids are not indicated.

vignette:
A 42-year-old female developed urticaria and respiratory distress during elective surgery. The anesthesiologist is requesting urgent allergy consultation.

Vital Signs
BP 82/50
HR 128
RR 28
Temp 98.2°F
SpO₂ 88%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Latex-Induced Anaphylaxis is the most likely diagnosis. OR nurse x 15 years + contact dermatitis from gloves + latex-fruit syndrome + reaction during mucosal exposure = classic latex anaphylaxis.
reasoning: Not asking about healthcare occupation and glove exposure
Missing latex-fruit syndrome history (banana, avocado, kiwi)
Not recognizing contact dermatitis from gloves as precursor
Delayed recognition of latex as cause during surgery

evidence.title: WAO Anaphylaxis Guidance 2020
evidence.cite: Cardona V, et al. World Allergy Organ J. 2020;13:100472
points: Epinephrine IM is first-line treatment - give immediately
Position patient supine with legs elevated (unless respiratory distress)
IV fluids for hypotension
H1 and H2 blockers, corticosteroids are adjunctive

illnessScript.epidemiology: Lifetime prevalence 1-2%. Common triggers: foods (peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish), medications (antibiotics, NSAIDs), Hymenoptera venom, latex. Idiopathic in 20% of cases. Fatality rate ~0.3% of hospital-treated cases.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: IgE-mediated mast cell/basophil degranulation → massive histamine release + tryptase + prostaglandins + leukotrienes → vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, bronchospasm, mucus secretion → distributive shock, airway compromise.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 82/50, HR 128, RR 28, SpO2 88% on 100% FiO2 - anaphylactic shock; Intubated, high airway pressures. Angioedema of lips noted.; Diffuse urticaria on trunk and extremities. Flushing.
illnessScript.workup: Clinical diagnosis based on rapid onset multisystem involvement. Serum tryptase if diagnosis uncertain (peaks 1-2h, obtain within 4h). Post-event: referral for skin testing/specific IgE to identify trigger.
illnessScript.treatment: Epinephrine IM (0.3-0.5mg, 1:1000) FIRST - no absolute contraindications. Repeat q5-15min PRN. Adjuncts: IV fluids, H1/H2 blockers, steroids, bronchodilators. Observe 4-6h minimum (biphasic reactions in 5-20%).

distractors.explanation: Neuromuscular blockers are common cause of OR anaphylaxis.
Cefazolin reactions usually occur sooner after administration.

vignette:
A 42-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "I was stung by a bee 3 weeks ago and had a severe reaction. My doctor said I should see an allergist."

Vital Signs
BP 128/82
HR 72
RR 14
Temp 98.4°F
SpO₂ 99%
stem: When is the optimal time to perform venom skin testing after a systemic reaction?

options.text: Testing is not needed if the clinical history is clear

teaching.keyPoint: Correct. This allows time for IgE antibodies to replenish after the anaphylactic event.
reasoning: Too soon - IgE may be depleted, leading to false negatives.
Unnecessarily delayed. Testing at 4-6 weeks is sufficient.
Testing is important to confirm the specific venom(s) and guide immunotherapy.

evidence.title: AAAAI Insect Sting Allergy Practice Parameter 2016
evidence.cite: Golden DBK, et al. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2017;139(4):1024-1044
points: Skin testing is gold standard for diagnosis (wait 4-6 weeks after reaction)
Venom immunotherapy (VIT) is 97% effective in preventing future reactions
VIT indicated for systemic reactions + positive venom testing

illnessScript.epidemiology: Systemic reactions in 0.4-0.8% of children, 3% of adults after Hymenoptera sting. 40-100 deaths/year in US. Risk factors for severe reaction: prior systemic reaction, older age, cardiovascular disease, beta-blocker/ACE-I use, mastocytosis.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Initial sting sensitizes → IgE antibodies to venom proteins (phospholipase, hyaluronidase, melittin). Re-sting → IgE crosslinking → mast cell degranulation → anaphylaxis. Large local reactions are IgE-mediated but rarely progress to systemic.
illnessScript.workup: Confirm IgE-mediated reaction: skin testing (gold standard) or serum specific IgE 4-6 weeks post-reaction. Baseline serum tryptase to screen for mastocytosis (elevated increases anaphylaxis risk).
illnessScript.treatment: Acute: epinephrine, supportive care. Long-term: prescribe epinephrine auto-injector. Venom immunotherapy (VIT) highly effective (97% protection), indicated for systemic reactions with confirmed IgE. Continue VIT for 3-5 years minimum.

distractors.explanation: Too soon - IgE may be depleted, leading to false negatives.
Unnecessarily delayed. Testing at 4-6 weeks is sufficient.

distractors.text: Testing is not needed if the clinical history is clear
distractors.explanation: Testing is important to confirm the specific venom(s) and guide immunotherapy.

vignette:
A 45-year-old female presents to the clinic, stating: "I need to find out if I'm really allergic to penicillin. I've had it listed as an allergy my whole life."

Vital Signs
BP 118/74
HR 68
RR 14
Temp 98.2°F
SpO₂ 99%
stem: A patient reports developing a 'rash' after amoxicillin as a child 30+ years ago. What is the probability they are TRULY allergic today?

teaching.keyPoint: Correct! Up to 80% of penicillin-allergic patients lose sensitivity within 10 years. With a vague childhood history 30+ years ago, true allergy is very unlikely (<5%).
reasoning: Actually the opposite - most lose sensitivity over time.
Still too high - most childhood reactions resolve.
While testing confirms, we can estimate probability. Vague remote history is very low risk.

evidence.title: AAAAI/ACAAI Drug Allergy Practice Parameter 2022
evidence.cite: Khan DA, et al. Ann Allergy Asthma Immunol. 2022;129(4):S1-S42
points: 90% of patients labeled penicillin allergic are NOT truly allergic
Penicillin skin testing is highly predictive (negative predictive value >95%)
Direct oral challenge safe after negative skin testing

illnessScript.epidemiology: Reported in 10-20% of hospitalized patients, but true allergy in only 10% of those reporting. Penicillin allergy most common (claimed by 10% of population, confirmed in <5%). Cross-reactivity between penicillins and cephalosporins ~2%.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Type I (immediate): IgE-mediated, minutes to 1 hour, urticaria/anaphylaxis. Type IV (delayed): T-cell mediated, days to weeks, maculopapular rash to severe cutaneous reactions (SJS/TEN, DRESS). Drug acts as hapten binding to proteins.
illnessScript.workup: Detailed history: timing, symptoms, prior tolerance, subsequent tolerance. Skin testing for immediate reactions (penicillin has validated reagents). Patch testing for delayed reactions. Drug challenge if low risk.
illnessScript.treatment: Acute: stop drug, treat symptoms (antihistamines for urticaria, epinephrine for anaphylaxis). Document allergy accurately. Consider desensitization if drug is essential and no severe cutaneous reaction history.

distractors.explanation: Actually the opposite - most lose sensitivity over time.
Still too high - most childhood reactions resolve.
While testing confirms, we can estimate probability. Vague remote history is very low risk.

vignette:
A 35-year-old female presents to the clinic, stating: "I've had hives every day for the past 3 months and I can't figure out what's causing them."

Vital Signs
BP 122/78
HR 74
RR 14
Temp 98.4°F
SpO₂ 99%
stem: A patient has daily hives for 3 months with no clear trigger. Allergy skin testing to foods and environmental allergens is most likely to show:

options.text: The specific allergen causing the hives
Negative or clinically irrelevant positive results
Environmental allergies that can be treated with immunotherapy
Multiple food allergies requiring elimination diet

teaching.keyPoint: Correct! Allergy testing in CSU is typically negative or shows sensitizations unrelated to urticaria. Guidelines recommend AGAINST routine allergy testing in chronic urticaria.
reasoning: Allergy testing rarely identifies triggers in chronic spontaneous urticaria.
Food allergy rarely causes chronic urticaria. Elimination diets are not recommended.
Environmental allergies don't cause chronic urticaria. Immunotherapy won't help.

evidence.title: International EAACI/GA2LEN/EDF/WAO Guideline 2022
evidence.cite: Zuberbier T, et al. Allergy. 2022;77(3):734-766
points: Chronic urticaria defined as daily or near-daily hives for ≥6 weeks
Most cases are chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU) - no identifiable trigger
Limited laboratory workup recommended: CBC, ESR/CRP. Extensive testing rarely helpful.

illnessScript.epidemiology: Chronic urticaria (>6 weeks) affects 0.5-1% of population. 80-90% are chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU) with no identifiable trigger. Female predominance 2:1. Average duration 2-5 years, but may persist longer.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Mast cell activation → histamine release → vasodilation, increased permeability → wheals. In CSU, often autoimmune: IgG autoantibodies against IgE or FcεRI receptor. Angioedema involves deeper dermis/subcutaneous tissue.
illnessScript.workup: Clinical diagnosis. Limited workup for CSU: CBC, CMP, TSH (thyroid autoimmunity associated). Consider autologous serum skin test for autoimmune subtype. Biopsy only if urticarial vasculitis suspected (lesions >24h, bruising, burning).
illnessScript.treatment: First-line: second-gen H1 antihistamine daily. Step up: increase to 4x standard dose. Add H2 blocker or LTRA if refractory. Omalizumab (anti-IgE) for antihistamine-refractory CSU. Avoid first-gen antihistamines (sedation).

distractors.text: The specific allergen causing the hives
distractors.explanation: Allergy testing rarely identifies triggers in chronic spontaneous urticaria.

distractors.text: Multiple food allergies requiring elimination diet
distractors.explanation: Food allergy rarely causes chronic urticaria. Elimination diets are not recommended.

distractors.text: Environmental allergies that can be treated with immunotherapy
distractors.explanation: Environmental allergies don't cause chronic urticaria. Immunotherapy won't help.

reasoning: Chronic steroids are NOT recommended due to side effects. Only short bursts for flares.

Omalizumab is reserved for patients who fail high-dose antihistamines.

vignette:
A 32-year-old female presents to the clinic, stating: "I keep getting sinus infections and pneumonia. My doctor said my immune system might be weak."

Vital Signs
BP 116/72
HR 76
RR 16
Temp 98.6°F
SpO₂ 97%
stem: A 32-year-old with recurrent sinusitis and 3 pneumonias in 2 years has IgG 280 mg/dL (normal 700-1600). What additional testing is needed before diagnosing CVID?

options.text: Genetic testing is required for diagnosis
No additional testing - low IgG confirms CVID
Check vaccine responses and exclude secondary causes

teaching.keyPoint: Correct! CVID requires both low immunoglobulins AND poor vaccine responses. Must exclude secondary causes (HIV, medications, protein loss, malignancy).
reasoning: Must also check vaccine responses and exclude secondary causes.
Genetic testing can support diagnosis but is not required. CVID is a clinical diagnosis.
Not routinely required for CVID diagnosis, though may be done if malignancy suspected.

evidence.title: AAAAI/ACAAI Practice Parameter: Immune Deficiency 2015
evidence.cite: Bonilla FA, et al. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2015;136(5):1186-1205
points: CVID is most common symptomatic primary immunodeficiency in adults
Diagnosis: Low IgG AND low IgA and/or IgM with poor vaccine responses
Must exclude secondary causes (medications, protein loss, malignancy)

illnessScript.epidemiology: Most common symptomatic primary immunodeficiency in adults. Prevalence 1 in 25,000-50,000. Usually diagnosed in 2nd-3rd decade. Equal sex distribution. Increased risk of autoimmunity (20-25%) and malignancy (lymphoma, gastric cancer).
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Defective B-cell differentiation → low IgG and IgA (±IgM) with poor vaccine responses. T-cell abnormalities also present. Genetic heterogeneity - multiple genes implicated. Results in recurrent sinopulmonary infections, GI infections, bronchiectasis.
illnessScript.workup: Low IgG AND low IgA or IgM, poor response to vaccine antigens (pneumococcal, tetanus), exclusion of secondary causes (medications, protein loss, malignancy). B-cell subsets often abnormal. Genetic testing available but not required for diagnosis.
illnessScript.treatment: IgG replacement therapy (IVIG or SCIG) - reduces infection frequency. Target trough IgG >500-800 mg/dL. Prophylactic antibiotics for breakthrough infections. Monitor for complications: bronchiectasis (chest CT), autoimmunity, malignancy.

distractors.text: No additional testing - low IgG confirms CVID
distractors.explanation: Must also check vaccine responses and exclude secondary causes.

distractors.text: Genetic testing is required for diagnosis
distractors.explanation: Genetic testing can support diagnosis but is not required. CVID is a clinical diagnosis.
Not routinely required for CVID diagnosis, though may be done if malignancy suspected.

stem: The patient is started on IVIG. What IgG trough level should be targeted?

options.text: Trough levels don't matter - just give standard dose
Greater than 500 mg/dL, higher if still having infections
Normal range (700-1600 mg/dL) must be achieved

teaching.keyPoint: Correct! Target trough >500 mg/dL minimum. Many patients need troughs of 700-800+ mg/dL to remain infection-free.
reasoning: Inadequate - patients need therapeutic levels to prevent infections.
While ideal, some patients do well with troughs slightly below normal range. Individualize based on infection frequency.
Trough levels guide dosing. Patients vary in IgG catabolism and requirement.

vignette:
A 45-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "My doctor found high eosinophils on my blood test and wants me to see a specialist."

Vital Signs
BP 132/84
HR 78
RR 14
Temp 98.4°F
SpO₂ 96%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Allergic Disease (Rhinitis, Asthma) is the most likely diagnosis. Common cause of mild-moderate eosinophilia. Patient has known allergies.
reasoning: Assuming allergies explain any eosinophilia (allergies rarely cause AEC >1500)
Not checking for parasites, especially in travelers/immigrants
Missing EGPA in asthma patients with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms
Not screening for organ damage in hypereosinophilia

evidence.title: Evaluation of Eosinophilia - UpToDate/Expert Review
points: Eosinophilia: >500/μL; Hypereosinophilia: >1500/μL persistently
Most common causes: Allergic disease, parasitic infection, drug reaction
Must-not-miss: EGPA (Churg-Strauss), hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES), malignancy
Organ damage screening essential for HE: cardiac (echo), pulmonary, skin, neuro

illnessScript.epidemiology: Defined as AEC >500/μL. Mild (500-1500), moderate (1500-5000), severe (>5000). Causes: allergic disease (most common in developed countries), parasitic infection (most common globally), drug reaction, eosinophilic GI disease, hypereosinophilic syndrome.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Eosinophils develop in bone marrow (IL-5 dependent) → migrate to tissues → release toxic granule proteins (MBP, ECP) → tissue damage. Eosinophilic infiltration causes organ dysfunction in hypereosinophilic syndrome (cardiac, pulmonary, neurologic).
illnessScript.keyFeatures: BP 132/84, HR 78, RR 14, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 96%. Blood pressure slightly elevated. O2 sat slightly low.; Well-appearing middle-aged man in no acute distress.; Nasal mucosa edematous and pale with inferior turbinate hypertrophy. Nasal polyps visualized bilaterally. Postnasal drip present.
illnessScript.workup: Peripheral smear, comprehensive history (medications, travel, allergies). Stool O&P if parasitic exposure. IgE level. If persistent/severe: bone marrow biopsy, FIP1L1-PDGFRA mutation (clonal variant), tryptase (r/o mastocytosis), organ-specific evaluation.
illnessScript.treatment: Treat underlying cause. Allergic: allergen avoidance, inhaled steroids if asthma. Parasitic: appropriate antiparasitic. HES: corticosteroids first-line, then imatinib (if PDGFRA+), mepolizumab, or other steroid-sparing agents. Monitor for end-organ damage.

distractors.explanation: Asthma + eosinophilia + neuropathy is classic triad. Potentially fatal vasculitis.
Persistent AEC >1500 with organ involvement. Requires cardiac screening.

vignette:
A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "I keep having episodes where I flush bright red, feel like I'm going to pass out, and get terrible diarrhea. My doctor found my tryptase is high."

BP 118/72
HR 82
RR 14
Temp 98.6°F
SpO₂ 98%
stem: A patient with recurrent flushing, diarrhea, and a baseline tryptase of 28 ng/mL (normal <11) should have which test?

options.text: Bone marrow biopsy with KIT mutation testing

teaching.keyPoint: Correct! Elevated baseline tryptase >20 ng/mL suggests systemic mastocytosis. Bone marrow biopsy is gold standard, looking for abnormal mast cell aggregates and KIT D816V mutation.
reasoning: Allergy testing won't diagnose mastocytosis.
May be done to look for organomegaly but won't diagnose mastocytosis.
This tests for pheochromocytoma - should be considered but bone marrow is priority given high tryptase.

evidence.title: Consensus on Mast Cell Disorders (ECNM/AIM)
evidence.cite: Valent P, et al. Int Arch Allergy Immunol. 2020;181(1):1-10
points: Systemic Mastocytosis: Baseline tryptase >20 ng/mL, KIT D816V mutation, abnormal mast cells on biopsy
MCAS: Episodic symptoms, tryptase rise >20% + 2 ng/mL during episode, response to mast cell mediator therapy
Bone marrow biopsy gold standard for mastocytosis diagnosis

illnessScript.epidemiology: Mastocytosis: 1 in 10,000. Includes cutaneous (urticaria pigmentosa, children) and systemic (adults). KIT D816V mutation in >90% of systemic cases. Anaphylaxis risk increased, especially with Hymenoptera stings.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Clonal mast cell proliferation → accumulation in tissues (skin, bone marrow, GI, liver, spleen) → spontaneous or triggered mediator release → flushing, pruritus, anaphylaxis, GI symptoms. Tryptase reflects mast cell burden.
illnessScript.workup: Serum tryptase (elevated, especially >20 ng/mL). Bone marrow biopsy (multifocal mast cell aggregates, >25% atypical, CD25+). KIT mutation analysis. Consider if recurrent unexplained anaphylaxis, especially with hypotension.
illnessScript.treatment: Avoid triggers (heat, alcohol, NSAIDs, opioids, certain anesthetics). H1/H2 antihistamines, cromolyn for GI symptoms. Epinephrine auto-injector mandatory. Cytoreductive therapy for advanced SM. KIT inhibitors (avapritinib, midostaurin) for advanced disease.

distractors.explanation: Allergy testing won't diagnose mastocytosis.
May be done to look for organomegaly but won't diagnose mastocytosis.
This tests for pheochromocytoma - should be considered but bone marrow is priority given high tryptase.

vignette:
An 8-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "Our son had hives and vomiting after eating something with peanuts. We need to know if he's really allergic."

Vital Signs
BP 98/62
HR 88
RR 18
Temp 98.4°F
SpO₂ 99%
stem: An 8-year-old had hives and vomiting within 30 minutes of eating peanut butter. Peanut skin test is 12mm wheal. What is the MOST accurate statement?

options.text: The large skin test confirms peanut allergy
This is likely coincidental and peanut can be reintroduced
The clinical history of immediate reaction plus positive skin test strongly supports IgE-mediated peanut allergy
An oral food challenge is required before diagnosis

teaching.keyPoint: Correct! The combination of convincing history (immediate symptoms after ingestion) plus positive testing = high probability of true allergy. Oral food challenge could confirm but is often not needed with clear history.
reasoning: Skin test shows sensitization, not clinical allergy. However, combined with convincing history, diagnosis is likely.
With clear history and positive testing, OFC is not required and would be risky.
Immediate reaction with hives and vomiting after peanut exposure is highly consistent with IgE-mediated allergy.

evidence.title: NIAID Food Allergy Guidelines 2010/Addendum 2017
evidence.cite: Togias A, et al. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2017;139(1):29-44
points: Oral food challenge is gold standard for diagnosis
Skin prick test and specific IgE help risk stratification but don't confirm allergy
Component testing (Ara h 2) improves specificity for peanut

illnessScript.epidemiology: Peanut allergy affects 2-3% of children in Western countries, prevalence increasing. Risk factors: family history of atopy, early eczema, egg allergy. Most common cause of food-induced anaphylaxis.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: IgE-mediated: sensitization to peanut proteins (Ara h 1, 2, 3) leads to mast cell degranulation on re-exposure. Ara h 2-specific IgE most predictive of clinical reactivity. Symptoms range from oral allergy syndrome to anaphylaxis.
illnessScript.workup: Skin prick test and/or serum specific IgE. Component testing (Ara h 2) improves specificity. Oral food challenge is gold standard for diagnosis but carries risk. Baseline tryptase to rule out mastocytosis.
illnessScript.treatment: Strict avoidance (read all labels). Epinephrine auto-injector mandatory for all patients. Oral immunotherapy (OIT) FDA-approved for desensitization. Early introduction (LEAP study) prevents allergy in high-risk infants.

distractors.text: The large skin test confirms peanut allergy
distractors.explanation: Skin test shows sensitization, not clinical allergy. However, combined with convincing history, diagnosis is likely.

distractors.text: An oral food challenge is required before diagnosis
distractors.explanation: With clear history and positive testing, OFC is not required and would be risky.

distractors.text: This is likely coincidental and peanut can be reintroduced
distractors.explanation: Immediate reaction with hives and vomiting after peanut exposure is highly consistent with IgE-mediated allergy.

stem: The child is confirmed to have peanut allergy. The parents ask if they should also avoid all tree nuts. What is the best response?

options.text: Yes, always avoid all tree nuts with peanut allergy
Only avoid tree nuts if they've had a reaction
Test for tree nut sensitization; avoid only those with positive tests and/or reactions
Tree nuts are safe since peanut is a legume

A 28-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "Food keeps getting stuck in my throat when I eat. I had to go to the ER because I couldn't swallow a piece of steak."

Vital Signs
BP 118/76
HR 72
RR 14
Temp 98.4°F
SpO₂ 99%
stem: A 28-year-old with dysphagia has EGD showing rings and furrows. Biopsy: 45 eos/hpf. Diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Correct! ≥15 eos/hpf + symptoms + endoscopic findings = EOE.
reasoning: GERD rarely causes >15 eos/hpf.
Would show fungi on biopsy, not eosinophils.
Different mechanism - would show aperistalsis.

evidence.title: AGA/JTF EOE Guidelines 2020
evidence.cite: Dellon ES, et al. Gastroenterology. 2020;158(6):1776-1786
points: EOE: ≥15 eosinophils/hpf on esophageal biopsy + symptoms
Three first-line treatments: PPI, swallowed steroids, elimination diet
Must biopsy even normal-appearing esophagus

illnessScript.epidemiology: Prevalence 50 per 100,000, increasing. Peak in young adult males. Strong association with atopy (50-80% have allergic rhinitis, asthma, or eczema). Food triggers common (milk, wheat, egg, soy, nuts, seafood).
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Chronic Th2-mediated immune response to food/environmental antigens → eosinophil recruitment to esophageal epithelium → inflammation, remodeling, fibrosis → dysphagia, food impaction, stricture formation.
illnessScript.workup: EGD with esophageal biopsies (≥15 eos/hpf in ≥1 biopsy, after 8-week PPI trial). Endoscopic findings: rings, furrows, white exudates, strictures. Allergy evaluation (skin prick testing, patch testing) to identify triggers.
illnessScript.treatment: Dietary elimination (6-food elimination or targeted based on testing). PPI (can treat PPI-responsive esophageal eosinophilia). Topical steroids (swallowed fluticasone or budesonide). Dilation for strictures. Dupilumab FDA-approved for refractory EoE.

distractors.explanation: GERD rarely causes >15 eos/hpf.
Would show fungi on biopsy, not eosinophils.
Different mechanism - would show aperistalsis.

Temp 98.6°F
SpO₂ 99%
stem: Recurrent angioedema WITHOUT urticaria, family history positive, antihistamines don't work. Best initial test?

teaching.keyPoint: Correct! C4 is low in HAE even BETWEEN attacks. Best screening test.
reasoning: HAE is not IgE-mediated.
For mast cell disorders, not HAE.
Won't work - HAE is bradykinin-mediated.

evidence.title: WAO/EAACI HAE Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Maurer M, et al. Allergy. 2022;77(7):1961-1990
points: HAE: recurrent angioedema WITHOUT urticaria
C4 is best screening test - low even between attacks
Bradykinin-mediated - antihistamines/steroids DON'T work

illnessScript.epidemiology: Rare: 1 in 50,000. Autosomal dominant C1-inhibitor deficiency (Type 1: low level, Type 2: normal level but dysfunctional). Type 3 (HAE with normal C1-INH) estrogen-dependent, affects women. Attacks often begin in childhood/adolescence.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: C1-INH deficiency → uncontrolled contact system activation → excess bradykinin production → increased vascular permeability → angioedema. Unlike histamine-mediated angioedema, does NOT respond to antihistamines, epinephrine, or steroids.
illnessScript.workup: C4 level (screening - low during and between attacks), C1-INH level and function, C1q (normal in HAE, low in acquired angioedema). Family history important. AAE if onset >40 years, consider underlying lymphoproliferative disorder.
illnessScript.treatment: Acute: C1-INH concentrate, icatibant (bradykinin B2 antagonist), or ecallantide (kallikrein inhibitor). NOT epinephrine/antihistamines/steroids. Prophylaxis: C1-INH, lanadelumab (anti-kallikrein mAb), berotralstat (oral kallikrein inhibitor).

distractors.explanation: HAE is not IgE-mediated.
For mast cell disorders, not HAE.
Won't work - HAE is bradykinin-mediated.

vignette:
A 42-year-old female presents to the clinic, stating: "I have this itchy rash on my hands that won't go away. It's been months and nothing helps."

Vital Signs
BP 120/78
HR 72
RR 14
Temp 98.4°F
SpO₂ 99%
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Irritant Contact Dermatitis is the most likely diagnosis. Wet work and chemical exposure can cause irritant dermatitis - may coexist.
reasoning: Treating with steroids indefinitely without identifying cause
Not considering occupational exposures
Missing common allergens (fragrance, preservatives in 'hypoallergenic' products)
Not doing comprehensive patch testing

evidence.title: ACDS Contact Dermatitis Guidelines
evidence.cite: American Contact Dermatitis Society Guidelines
points: Patch testing is gold standard for identifying contact allergens
Most common allergens: nickel, fragrance, preservatives, rubber
Irritant vs allergic: irritant is dose-dependent, allergic is immune-mediated
Treatment: allergen avoidance, topical steroids, emollients

illnessScript.epidemiology: Allergic contact dermatitis affects 15-20% of population. Common allergens: nickel (most common), fragrance, preservatives (formaldehyde releasers), rubber chemicals, hair dye (PPD), poison ivy (urushiol). Occupational exposure significant.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Type IV delayed hypersensitivity. Hapten (low MW chemical) binds skin proteins → Langerhans cells process antigen → migrate to lymph nodes → sensitize T cells. Re-exposure: memory T cells → cytokine release → eczematous inflammation at 24-72 hours.
illnessScript.keyFeatures: Bilateral hand dermatitis: erythema, scaling, fissures on dorsal hands and web spaces. Some vesicles on fingers. Lichenification from chronic scratching. Worse on dominant hand.; Forearms clear. No dermatitis elsewhere. No involvement of palms (spared).
illnessScript.workup: Clinical diagnosis based on morphology and distribution (geometric, linear suggests external contact). Patch testing to identify specific allergen - apply standardized panels for 48h, read at 48h and 96h. Consider relevance of positive reactions.
illnessScript.treatment: Avoidance of identified allergen (most important). Acute: topical steroids, wet dressings. Severe/widespread: systemic steroids short course. Emollients for barrier repair. Occupational cases may require job modification.

distractors.explanation: No childhood eczema history. Adult-onset hand eczema is usually contact-related.
Can affect hands but usually has other features (nail pitting, plaques elsewhere).

vignette:
A 35-year-old male presents to the emergency department, stating: "I have a rash all over my body and high fever. I started a new seizure medication about 3 weeks ago."

Vital Signs
BP 108/68
HR 112
RR 18
Temp 103.2°F
SpO₂ 96%
stem: Patient started phenytoin 3 weeks ago, now has fever, diffuse rash, facial swelling, and eosinophilia. What is the MOST important immediate action?

teaching.keyPoint: Correct! Stopping the culprit drug is the most critical intervention. DRESS has 10% mortality, mainly from liver failure.
reasoning: WRONG - must stop the culprit drug immediately.
Carbamazepine has high cross-reactivity with phenytoin - contraindicated.
Delay is dangerous - stop drug immediately.

evidence.title: RegiSCAR DRESS Diagnostic Criteria
evidence.cite: Kardaun SH, et al. Br J Dermatol. 2013;169(5):1071-1080
points: DRESS: Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms
Onset 2-8 weeks after starting culprit drug
Features: Fever, rash, eosinophilia, lymphadenopathy, organ involvement (liver, kidney, lung)

illnessScript.epidemiology: Reported in 10-20% of hospitalized patients, but true allergy in only 10% of those reporting. Penicillin allergy most common (claimed by 10% of population, confirmed in <5%). Cross-reactivity between penicillins and cephalosporins ~2%.
illnessScript.pathophysiology: Type I (immediate): IgE-mediated, minutes to 1 hour, urticaria/anaphylaxis. Type IV (delayed): T-cell mediated, days to weeks, maculopapular rash to severe cutaneous reactions (SJS/TEN, DRESS). Drug acts as hapten binding to proteins.
illnessScript.workup: Detailed history: timing, symptoms, prior tolerance, subsequent tolerance. Skin testing for immediate reactions (penicillin has validated reagents). Patch testing for delayed reactions. Drug challenge if low risk.
illnessScript.treatment: Acute: stop drug, treat symptoms (antihistamines for urticaria, epinephrine for anaphylaxis). Document allergy accurately. Consider desensitization if drug is essential and no severe cutaneous reaction history.

distractors.explanation: WRONG - must stop the culprit drug immediately.
Carbamazepine has high cross-reactivity with phenytoin - contraindicated.
Delay is dangerous - stop drug immediately.

vignette:
A 45-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "My left leg has been hurting for 2 days." She reports that symptoms began 5 days ago and have been progressively worsening.

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She never smoked; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a homemaker; lives with spouse.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 99.2°F
HR 88 bpm
BP 132/82 mm Hg
RR 16/min
SpO₂ 98%

Physical Examination:
General: anxious-appearing, mildly diaphoretic
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Lungs: Bibasilar crackles, no wheezing
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; 3/6 systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur at the right upper sternal border radiating to the carotids
Extremities: 1+ bilateral lower extremity edema to the mid-shins

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 8.3 × 10³/µL, Hgb 14.5 g/dL, Plt 218 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 137 mEq/L, K 3.8 mEq/L, Cl 102 mEq/L, CO₂ 27 mEq/L, BUN 12 mg/dL, Cr 0.9 mg/dL, Glucose 123 mg/dL
Cardiac markers: Troponin I 0.27 ng/mL (ref <0.04), BNP 691 pg/mL

Imaging:
ECG: Sinus tachycardia at 108 bpm; ST depressions in leads V4-V6 and II, III, aVF; no ST elevations
Chest X-ray: Cardiomegaly with cephalization of pulmonary vessels; bilateral pleural effusions, left greater than right
stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which diagnosis is most consistent with the findings?

options.text: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response

teaching.keyPoint: Deep Vein Thrombosis is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of My left leg has been hurting for 2 days, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Not using Wells score to risk-stratify before testing
Ordering D-dimer in high-probability patients (will likely be positive regardless)
Accepting negative D-dimer without considering pre-test probability
Diagnosing cellulitis without considering DVT when risk factors present

platform.title: Practice Case: GI and Neurological Symptoms

vignette:
A 68-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "I can't catch my breath." He reports that symptoms began 6 days ago and have been progressively worsening. He describes a productive cough with yellow-green sputum, associated fevers and chills, and increasing shortness of breath with exertion.

Past Medical History: hypertension.

Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: He never smoked; reports drinking 1-2 beers daily; works as a homemaker; lives alone.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.2°F
HR 102 bpm
BP 158/94 mm Hg
RR 26/min
SpO₂ 89%

Physical Examination:
General: appears uncomfortable but in no acute distress
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Lungs: Bibasilar crackles, no wheezing
Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm; S3 gallop present; jugular venous distension to 10 cm
Extremities: 1+ bilateral lower extremity edema to the mid-shins

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 9.7 × 10³/µL, Hgb 13.3 g/dL, Plt 187 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 140 mEq/L, K 4.2 mEq/L, Cl 100 mEq/L, CO₂ 25 mEq/L, BUN 16 mg/dL, Cr 1.1 mg/dL, Glucose 93 mg/dL
Cardiac markers: Troponin I 0.31 ng/mL (ref <0.04), BNP 859 pg/mL

Imaging:
ECG: Sinus tachycardia at 108 bpm; ST depressions in leads V4-V6 and II, III, aVF; no ST elevations
Chest X-ray: Cardiomegaly with cephalization of pulmonary vessels; bilateral pleural effusions, left greater than right
stem: The combination of history, physical examination, and diagnostic studies is most suggestive of which condition?

teaching.keyPoint: Acute Decompensated Heart Failure is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of I can't catch my breath, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Not identifying the precipitant (dietary indiscretion, medication non-compliance, ACS, arrhythmia, infection)
Giving excessive IV fluids to a volume-overloaded patient
Not obtaining BNP/NT-proBNP to help differentiate cardiac from pulmonary dyspnea
Missing concurrent ACS - always get ECG and troponin

Temp 98.8°F

SpO₂ 94%

reasoning: Cardioverting new-onset AF without anticoagulation when duration >48 hours - stroke risk!
Not looking for underlying triggers: infection, PE, MI, thyroid disease, alcohol
Forgetting to calculate CHA2DS2-VASc score for stroke risk
Over-aggressive rate control causing hypotension

platform.title: Practice Case: Worsening Shortness of Breath

vignette:
A 58-year-old neutral presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "Chest discomfort for two weeks." She reports that symptoms began 2 days ago and have been progressively worsening. She describes the pain as substernal, pressure-like, radiating to the left arm, and associated with diaphoresis and nausea.

Past Medical History: hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia.

Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She never smoked; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a retired teacher; lives with spouse.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.4°F
HR 94 bpm
BP 136/86 mm Hg
RR 18/min
SpO₂ 97%

Physical Examination:
General: anxious-appearing, mildly diaphoretic
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Lungs: Bibasilar crackles, no wheezing
Cardiovascular: Irregularly irregular rhythm, rate approximately 110; 2/6 systolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla
Extremities: 1+ bilateral lower extremity edema to the mid-shins

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 7.5 × 10³/µL, Hgb 12.7 g/dL, Plt 321 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 138 mEq/L, K 4.3 mEq/L, Cl 102 mEq/L, CO₂ 22 mEq/L, BUN 13 mg/dL, Cr 1.1 mg/dL, Glucose 115 mg/dL
Cardiac markers: Troponin I 0.50 ng/mL (ref <0.04), BNP 1026 pg/mL

Imaging:
ECG: Sinus tachycardia at 108 bpm; ST depressions in leads V4-V6 and II, III, aVF; no ST elevations
Chest X-ray: Cardiomegaly with cephalization of pulmonary vessels; bilateral pleural effusions, left greater than right
stem: Which of the following best explains this patient's presentation?

teaching.keyPoint: Acute Coronary Syndrome / Unstable Angina is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Chest discomfort for two weeks, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Assuming 'burning' chest pain is always GI - cardiac ischemia can present as burning, especially in diabetics
Being reassured by negative initial troponin - serial troponins are essential; early troponin can be negative
Attributing symptoms to GERD because patient had some relief with antacids - nitroglycerin relief is not specific for cardiac pain, and neither is antacid relief
Missing the 'crescendo pattern' - symptoms getting worse or occurring with less exertion defines unstable angina

evidence.title: AHA/ACC Chest Pain Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Gulati M, et al. Circulation. 2021;144:e364-e454
points: High-sensitivity troponin preferred for acute chest pain evaluation
Risk stratification using validated tools (HEART, TIMI, GRACE)
ECG within 10 minutes of arrival for suspected ACS
Serial troponins at 0 and 3 hours (or 0 and 1 hour for hs-cTn)

Social History: He quit smoking 10 years ago after a 20-pack-year history; reports drinking 1-2 beers daily; works as a nurse; lives alone.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.2°F
HR 72 bpm
BP 118/78 mm Hg
RR 16/min
SpO₂ 99%

Physical Examination:
General: appears uncomfortable but in no acute distress
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Lungs: Bibasilar crackles, no wheezing
Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm; S3 gallop present; jugular venous distension to 10 cm
Extremities: 1+ bilateral lower extremity edema to the mid-shins

options.text: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response

teaching.keyPoint: Aortic Stenosis is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of I passed out while climbing stairs, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Assuming all syncope in young adults is vasovagal - EXERTIONAL syncope is always concerning for cardiac cause
Dismissing a 'childhood murmur' as benign - bicuspid aortic valve is the #1 cause of AS in patients under 65
Not performing dynamic auscultation - Valsalva and standing maneuvers distinguish AS from HOCM
Forgetting the AS triad: angina, syncope, dyspnea - once symptomatic, prognosis is poor without intervention

evidence.cite: Shen WK, et al. Circulation. 2017;136:e60-e122
points: History, physical exam, and ECG for initial evaluation (Class I)
Risk stratify: high-risk features include structural heart disease, abnormal ECG, syncope during exertion
Echocardiography when cardiac cause suspected
Tilt table testing for recurrent unexplained syncope

teaching.keyPoint: CHF Exacerbation (Systolic Heart Failure) is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of I get short of breath really easily lately, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Missing medication non-adherence as the trigger - ALWAYS ask about adherence in CHF exacerbations
Forgetting that NSAIDs (ibuprofen) worsen heart failure by causing fluid retention
Not considering PE just because patient has known CHF - recent flight + HRT = VTE risk factors
Forgetting that ACS can trigger CHF decompensation - always check for ischemia

evidence.cite: Heidenreich PA, et al. Circulation. 2022;145:e895-e1032
points: Assess volume status and congestion
IV diuretics for acute decompensation
Identify and treat precipitating factors
Optimize GDMT before discharge

Social History: He never smoked; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a truck driver; lives with spouse.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.4°F
HR 78 bpm
BP 156/98 mm Hg
RR 14/min
SpO₂ 99%

Physical Examination:
General: alert and oriented, appears ill
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Lungs: Bibasilar crackles, no wheezing
Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm; S3 gallop present; jugular venous distension to 10 cm
Extremities: 1+ bilateral lower extremity edema to the mid-shins

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 7.0 × 10³/µL, Hgb 13.7 g/dL, Plt 200 × 10³/µL

stem: The combination of history, physical examination, and diagnostic studies is most suggestive of which condition?

teaching.keyPoint: Essential (Primary) Hypertension is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of I was told my blood pressure was high at a health fair, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Dismissing 'white coat hypertension' without confirming with home or ambulatory BP monitoring
Not screening for secondary causes in anyone <30 or >55 at diagnosis, or with resistant/severe HTN
Forgetting to check BP in BOTH arms at initial diagnosis - asymmetry suggests coarctation or vascular disease
Missing hypokalemia on labs which suggests primary aldosteronism

evidence.title: 2017 ACC/AHA Hypertension Guidelines
evidence.cite: Whelton PK, et al. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2018;71:e127-e248
points: Stage 2 HTN: BP ≥140/90 mmHg
Initial workup: CBC, CMP, lipid panel, TSH, urinalysis, ECG
Target organ damage assessment: LVH, retinopathy, nephropathy
Lifestyle modifications: DASH diet, sodium restriction, exercise, weight loss

teaching.keyPoint: Acute Pericarditis is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Sharp chest pain for 2 days, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Dismissing chest pain in young patient without full evaluation
Missing the positional nature of the pain (worse lying flat, better leaning forward)
Not hearing the pericardial friction rub (must listen with patient leaning forward)
Mistaking diffuse ST elevation for STEMI and activating cath lab

evidence.title: ESC Guidelines for Pericardial Diseases 2015
evidence.cite: Adler Y, et al. Eur Heart J. 2015;36:2921-2964
points: Diagnosis: 2 of 4 criteria (chest pain, friction rub, ECG changes, effusion)
NSAIDs + Colchicine is first-line therapy
Colchicine reduces recurrence risk by 50%
Avoid corticosteroids initially (increase recurrence)

stem: Which of the following best explains this patient's presentation?

teaching.keyPoint: Allergic Contact Dermatitis is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Itchy red rash on my hands for about 2 weeks, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.

Missing subtle signs of secondary infection
Prescribing topical steroids without addressing the cause
Not considering scabies in itchy rash with appropriate exposure

points: Identify and eliminate the offending agent
Patch testing for chronic or recurrent dermatitis
Topical corticosteroids are mainstay of treatment
High-potency steroids for limited areas, not face/folds

vignette:
A 52-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "I've been exhausted for months and I keep feeling dizzy when I stand up." She reports that symptoms began 3 days ago and have been progressively worsening.

Past Medical History: hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, empagliflozin 10 mg daily.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She current smoker with a 30-pack-year history; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a retired teacher; lives with spouse.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.0°F
HR 98 bpm
BP 92/58 mm Hg
RR 16/min
SpO₂ 99%

Physical Examination:
General: anxious-appearing, mildly diaphoretic
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 6.4 × 10³/µL, Hgb 14.0 g/dL, Plt 299 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.2 mEq/L, Cl 98 mEq/L, CO₂ 27 mEq/L, BUN 21 mg/dL, Cr 1.2 mg/dL, Glucose 96 mg/dL
stem: The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following diagnoses?

teaching.keyPoint: Primary Adrenal Insufficiency (Addison's) is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of I've been exhausted for months and I keep feeling dizzy when I stand up, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Missing the diagnosis - symptoms are vague (fatigue, weight loss, dizziness)
Not checking AM cortisol in unexplained hypotension or hyponatremia
Forgetting to give stress-dose steroids during illness/surgery
Not recognizing hyperpigmentation as clue to PRIMARY adrenal insufficiency

platform.title: Practice Case: Fatigue and Feeling Weak

vignette:
A 28-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "I've been throwing up all day, my stomach hurts, and I feel really weak." He reports that symptoms began 2 days ago and have been progressively worsening.

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: He current smoker with a 30-pack-year history; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a office worker; lives with spouse.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 99.1°F
HR 118 bpm
BP 102/68 mm Hg
RR 26/min
SpO₂ 99%

Physical Examination:
General: fatigued-appearing but cooperative
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 9.6 × 10³/µL, Hgb 13.6 g/dL, Plt 230 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 139 mEq/L, K 3.9 mEq/L, Cl 101 mEq/L, CO₂ 22 mEq/L, BUN 20 mg/dL, Cr 1.1 mg/dL, Glucose 117 mg/dL
stem: The combination of history, physical examination, and diagnostic studies is most suggestive of which condition?

teaching.keyPoint: Diabetic Ketoacidosis is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of I've been throwing up all day, my stomach hurts, and I feel really weak, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Not giving insulin early enough - IV fluids alone won't correct DKA
Not replacing potassium - insulin shifts K+ intracellularly, causing dangerous hypokalemia
Stopping insulin when glucose normalizes - continue until anion gap closes
Not looking for precipitating cause (Infection, Infarction, Insulin missed, etc.)

vignette:
A 38-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "I've been peeing a lot and I'm always thirsty." She reports that symptoms began 6 days ago and have been progressively worsening.

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: Penicillin (rash).

Social History: She never smoked; reports drinking 1-2 beers daily; works as a truck driver; lives with spouse.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.6°F
HR 104 bpm
BP 102/68 mm Hg
RR 18/min
SpO₂ 99%

Physical Examination:
General: anxious-appearing, mildly diaphoretic
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 10.0 × 10³/µL, Hgb 14.4 g/dL, Plt 327 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.0 mEq/L, Cl 99 mEq/L, CO₂ 25 mEq/L, BUN 12 mg/dL, Cr 0.9 mg/dL, Glucose 123 mg/dL
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of I've been peeing a lot and I'm always thirsty, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Attributing polyuria to 'drinking too much water' without checking glucose - the thirst is CAUSED by hyperglycemia
Not recognizing the classic triad: polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss despite normal/increased appetite
Forgetting to check for DKA red flags: abdominal pain, vomiting, fruity breath, altered mental status
Missing the recurrent yeast infections as a clue to diabetes - glucose in urine feeds Candida

evidence.title: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes 2026 - Section 2: Diagnosis and Classification
evidence.cite: American Diabetes Association. Diabetes Care. 2026;49(Suppl 1):S27-S49
points: Diagnostic criteria: A1C ≥6.5% OR FPG ≥126 mg/dL OR 2-h PG ≥200 mg/dL OR random glucose ≥200 mg/dL with symptoms
Without hyperglycemic crisis, diagnosis requires two abnormal results (same or different tests)
Prediabetes: FPG 100-125 mg/dL, 2-h PG 140-199 mg/dL, or A1C 5.7-6.4%
Classification important for treatment: Type 1 vs Type 2 vs other specific types

vignette:
A 28-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "I haven't had my period in 4 months and I'm having weird discharge from my nipples." She reports that symptoms began 5 days ago and have been progressively worsening.

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She current smoker with a 30-pack-year history; denies alcohol use; works as a construction worker; lives alone.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.4°F
HR 72 bpm
BP 118/74 mm Hg
RR 14/min
SpO₂ 99%

Physical Examination:
General: alert and oriented, appears ill
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 8.2 × 10³/µL, Hgb 13.8 g/dL, Plt 254 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 137 mEq/L, K 4.2 mEq/L, Cl 100 mEq/L, CO₂ 25 mEq/L, BUN 13 mg/dL, Cr 1.1 mg/dL, Glucose 100 mg/dL
stem: Which of the following best explains this patient's presentation?

teaching.keyPoint: Hyperprolactinemia (Prolactinoma) is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of I haven't had my period in 4 months and I'm having weird discharge from my nipples, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Not getting pregnancy test FIRST - must rule out pregnancy before any other workup
Attributing amenorrhea to 'stress' without checking prolactin level
Missing the visual field defects on exam - pituitary tumors compress the optic chiasm
Not asking about galactorrhea - patients may not volunteer this symptom due to embarrassment

vignette:
A 36-year-old neutral presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "I've had this cough and congestion for about a week." She reports that symptoms began 4 days ago and have been progressively worsening. She describes a productive cough with yellow-green sputum, associated fevers and chills, and increasing shortness of breath with exertion.

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She never smoked; denies alcohol use; works as a homemaker; lives alone.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 99.3°F
HR 92 bpm
BP 116/74 mm Hg
RR 18/min
SpO₂ 98%

Physical Examination:
General: alert and oriented, appears ill
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 6.4 × 10³/µL, Hgb 15.0 g/dL, Plt 327 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 138 mEq/L, K 3.8 mEq/L, Cl 101 mEq/L, CO₂ 23 mEq/L, BUN 19 mg/dL, Cr 0.8 mg/dL, Glucose 128 mg/dL
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Viral Upper Respiratory Infection (URI) is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of I've had this cough and congestion for about a week, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Prescribing antibiotics for viral URI because 'the patient expects them' - this is harmful to the patient and society
Thinking yellow/green sputum means bacterial infection - COLOR DOES NOT INDICATE BACTERIA
Diagnosing 'bacterial sinusitis' without meeting criteria: >10 days OR double-worsening OR severe symptoms
Not educating patients about the natural course of viral URI (7-10 days is normal!)

platform.title: Practice Case: Can't Catch My Breath

vignette:
A 32-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "My eye is red and watery." He reports that symptoms began 4 days ago and have been progressively worsening.

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: Penicillin (rash).

Social History: He quit smoking 10 years ago after a 20-pack-year history; denies alcohol use; works as a retired teacher; lives alone.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.6°F
HR 72 bpm
BP 122/78 mm Hg
RR 14/min
SpO₂ 99%

Physical Examination:
General: fatigued-appearing but cooperative
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 6.5 × 10³/µL, Hgb 13.6 g/dL, Plt 293 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 141 mEq/L, K 4.3 mEq/L, Cl 102 mEq/L, CO₂ 24 mEq/L, BUN 16 mg/dL, Cr 0.9 mg/dL, Glucose 98 mg/dL
stem: The combination of history, physical examination, and diagnostic studies is most suggestive of which condition?

teaching.keyPoint: Viral Conjunctivitis is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of My eye is red and watery, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Not checking visual acuity
Missing photophobia which suggests deeper inflammation
Prescribing antibiotics for viral conjunctivitis

platform.title: Practice Case: GI and Neurological Symptoms

vignette:
A 65-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "Severe eye pain with blurred vision." She reports that symptoms began 2 days ago and have been progressively worsening. She describes the pain as substernal, pressure-like, radiating to the left arm, and associated with diaphoresis and nausea.

Past Medical History: type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She never smoked; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a truck driver; lives with spouse.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.4°F
HR 88 bpm
BP 158/92 mm Hg
RR 16/min
SpO₂ 98%

Physical Examination:
General: appears uncomfortable but in no acute distress
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 7.8 × 10³/µL, Hgb 14.4 g/dL, Plt 309 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 141 mEq/L, K 4.2 mEq/L, Cl 102 mEq/L, CO₂ 26 mEq/L, BUN 16 mg/dL, Cr 0.9 mg/dL, Glucose 121 mg/dL
stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which diagnosis is most consistent with the findings?

teaching.keyPoint: Acute Angle Closure Glaucoma is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Severe eye pain with blurred vision, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Dismissing as conjunctivitis without thorough exam
Not recognizing the fixed mid-dilated pupil
Missing halos around lights as key symptom
Delaying ophthalmology referral

evidence.title: AAO Acute Angle-Closure Crisis Guidelines
evidence.cite: American Academy of Ophthalmology
points: EMERGENCY - can cause permanent vision loss within hours
Symptoms: severe eye pain, headache, nausea, halos around lights
IOP lowering: topical beta-blockers, alpha-agonists, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors
IV mannitol or oral glycerin for refractory cases

platform.title: Practice Case: GI and Neurological Symptoms

vignette:
A 78-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "The nursing staff called because my mother has been very confused since last night." She reports that symptoms began 5 days ago and have been progressively worsening.

Past Medical History: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, osteoarthritis, benign prostatic hyperplasia.

Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She never smoked; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a office worker; lives in assisted living facility.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 99.8°F
HR 96 bpm
BP 148/82 mm Hg
RR 18/min
SpO₂ 93%

Physical Examination:
General: fatigued-appearing but cooperative
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 6.1 × 10³/µL, Hgb 13.5 g/dL, Plt 182 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 140 mEq/L, K 3.7 mEq/L, Cl 99 mEq/L, CO₂ 23 mEq/L, BUN 16 mg/dL, Cr 0.9 mg/dL, Glucose 119 mg/dL
stem: The combination of history, physical examination, and diagnostic studies is most suggestive of which condition?

teaching.keyPoint: Delirium (multifactorial) is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of The nursing staff called because my mother has been very confused since last night, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Assuming confusion is 'just dementia' or 'sundowning' without evaluation
Not obtaining collateral history to establish BASELINE mental status
Missing medication-induced delirium (anticholinergics, opioids, benzos)
Forgetting to check glucose and urinalysis in confused elderly

evidence.title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Evans L, et al. Crit Care Med. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143
points: Sepsis and septic shock are medical emergencies - treatment begins immediately (Best Practice)
Recommend against using qSOFA alone for screening - use NEWS, MEWS, or SIRS (Strong)
Measure blood lactate in adults suspected of sepsis (Weak)
At least 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid within first 3 hours for hypoperfusion (Weak)

platform.title: Practice Case: GI and Neurological Symptoms

vignette:
A 79-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "I keep falling - three times in the last month." She reports that symptoms began 5 days ago and have been progressively worsening.

Past Medical History: hypertension, osteoarthritis.

Medications: losartan 50 mg daily.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She current smoker with a 30-pack-year history; history of alcohol use disorder, sober for 5 years; works as a retired teacher; lives in assisted living facility.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.2°F
HR 72 bpm
BP 148/72 sitting, 118/68 standing mm Hg
RR 16/min
SpO₂ 97%

Physical Examination:
General: alert and oriented, appears ill
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 8.3 × 10³/µL, Hgb 14.3 g/dL, Plt 251 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 138 mEq/L, K 4.1 mEq/L, Cl 100 mEq/L, CO₂ 23 mEq/L, BUN 21 mg/dL, Cr 0.9 mg/dL, Glucose 123 mg/dL
stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which diagnosis is most consistent with the findings?

teaching.keyPoint: Gait/Balance Disorder is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of I keep falling - three times in the last month, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Not checking orthostatic vital signs
Not doing gait and balance assessment
Missing polypharmacy as contributor (especially sedatives, antihypertensives)
Not assessing vision and hearing

evidence.title: AGS/BGS Falls Prevention Guidelines 2011
evidence.cite: American Geriatrics Society. J Am Geriatr Soc. 2011;59:148-157
points: Screen all adults ≥65 annually for falls
Multifactorial assessment if recurrent falls or gait/balance issues
Review medications - reduce polypharmacy, avoid sedatives
Vitamin D supplementation (≥800 IU daily)

platform.title: Practice Case: GI and Neurological Symptoms

vignette:
A 58-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "I vomited blood this morning." He reports that symptoms began 3 days ago and have been progressively worsening.

Past Medical History: hyperlipidemia.

Medications: atorvastatin 40 mg daily.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: He quit smoking 10 years ago after a 20-pack-year history; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a construction worker; lives with spouse.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.4°F
HR 112 bpm
BP 98/62 mm Hg
RR 18/min
SpO₂ 97%

Physical Examination:
General: fatigued-appearing but cooperative
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs
Abdomen: Soft, tender in the right lower quadrant with voluntary guarding; positive McBurney point tenderness; negative Rovsing sign; no rebound tenderness
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 7.4 × 10³/µL, Hgb 13.5 g/dL, Plt 252 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 137 mEq/L, K 4.2 mEq/L, Cl 98 mEq/L, CO₂ 24 mEq/L, BUN 20 mg/dL, Cr 1.0 mg/dL, Glucose 99 mg/dL
LFTs: AST 105 U/L, ALT 192 U/L, Alk Phos 77 U/L, T. Bili 2.4 mg/dL
Lipase 494 U/L (ref 10-140)

Imaging:
CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast: Edematous pancreas with peripancreatic fat stranding; no necrosis or pseudocyst formation
stem: The combination of history, physical examination, and diagnostic studies is most suggestive of which condition?

teaching.keyPoint: Upper GI Bleeding (Peptic Ulcer Disease) is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of I vomited blood this morning, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Underestimating blood loss - orthostatic vitals and hematocrit can lag behind actual loss
Delaying resuscitation for diagnostic workup
Not calculating Glasgow-Blatchford score to risk-stratify
Missing signs of variceal bleeding (stigmata of cirrhosis) which requires different treatment

evidence.title: ACG Upper GI Bleeding Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Laine L, et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2021;116:899-917
points: Risk stratify with Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS)
GBS 0-1 may be candidates for outpatient management
IV PPI bolus then infusion for high-risk stigmata
EGD within 24 hours for most patients; 12 hours if hemodynamically unstable
