A 68-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "My stomach hurts and I keep vomiting." She reports that symptoms began 5 days ago and have been progressively worsening.

Past Medical History: type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia.

Medications: glipizide 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She quit smoking 3 years ago after a 15-pack-year history; denies alcohol use; works as a accountant; lives with spouse.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 99.2°F
HR 104 bpm
BP 108/72 mm Hg
RR 20/min
SpO₂ 97%

Physical Examination:
General: appears uncomfortable but in no acute distress
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs
Abdomen: Distended, diffusely tender with hypoactive bowel sounds; mild guarding in the epigastric region
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 7.0 × 10³/µL, Hgb 13.3 g/dL, Plt 248 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 136 mEq/L, K 4.3 mEq/L, Cl 103 mEq/L, CO₂ 25 mEq/L, BUN 21 mg/dL, Cr 1.0 mg/dL, Glucose 128 mg/dL
LFTs: AST 114 U/L, ALT 153 U/L, Alk Phos 86 U/L, T. Bili 2.1 mg/dL
Lipase 234 U/L (ref 10-140)

Imaging:

stem: The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following diagnoses?

teaching.keyPoint: Small Bowel Obstruction is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of My stomach hurts and I keep vomiting, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Missing signs of strangulation (fever, tachycardia, focal tenderness, peritonitis)
Delaying surgical consultation
Giving oral contrast when obstruction is complete
Not placing NG tube for decompression

vignette:
A 58-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "Burning stomach pain for 3 days." He reports that symptoms began 4 days ago and have been progressively worsening. He describes the pain as substernal, pressure-like, radiating to the left arm, and associated with diaphoresis and nausea.

Past Medical History: hypertension.

Medications: metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: He never smoked; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a truck driver; lives alone.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.6°F
HR 78 bpm
BP 145/88 mm Hg
RR 16/min
SpO₂ 98%

Physical Examination:
General: alert and oriented, appears ill
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs
Abdomen: Soft, tender in the right lower quadrant with voluntary guarding; positive McBurney point tenderness; negative Rovsing sign; no rebound tenderness
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 8.2 × 10³/µL, Hgb 13.1 g/dL, Plt 184 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.3 mEq/L, Cl 103 mEq/L, CO₂ 22 mEq/L, BUN 12 mg/dL, Cr 1.0 mg/dL, Glucose 108 mg/dL
LFTs: AST 82 U/L, ALT 95 U/L, Alk Phos 57 U/L, T. Bili 3.0 mg/dL
Lipase 478 U/L (ref 10-140)

Imaging:
CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast: Edematous pancreas with peripancreatic fat stranding; no necrosis or pseudocyst formation
stem: The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following diagnoses?

teaching.keyPoint: Peptic Ulcer Disease is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Burning stomach pain for 3 days, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Assuming 'burning' pain is always GERD - burning can occur with PUD too
Missing the dark stools (melena) which indicates upper GI bleeding
Not considering ACS in a middle-aged male with cardiac risk factors presenting with 'stomach pain'
Forgetting to ask about NSAID use - patients often don't consider OTC meds as 'medications'

evidence.title: ACG GERD Guidelines 2022
evidence.cite: Katz PO, et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2022;117:27-56
points: PPI for 8 weeks trial
EGD for alarm symptoms
Lifestyle modifications

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She quit smoking 3 years ago after a 15-pack-year history; denies alcohol use; works as a accountant; lives with spouse.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 97.5°F
HR 72 bpm
BP 118/75 mm Hg
RR 16/min
SpO₂ 99%

Physical Examination:
General: alert and oriented, appears ill
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs
Abdomen: Soft, tender in the right upper quadrant with a positive Murphy sign; no hepatomegaly
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 6.7 × 10³/µL, Hgb 12.0 g/dL, Plt 296 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 138 mEq/L, K 3.8 mEq/L, Cl 100 mEq/L, CO₂ 24 mEq/L, BUN 17 mg/dL, Cr 1.0 mg/dL, Glucose 129 mg/dL
LFTs: AST 178 U/L, ALT 201 U/L, Alk Phos 69 U/L, T. Bili 2.7 mg/dL
Lipase 297 U/L (ref 10-140)

Imaging:

stem: The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following diagnoses?

teaching.keyPoint: Hemorrhoids is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Blood on toilet paper, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Attributing rectal bleeding to hemorrhoids without colonoscopy - hemorrhoids don't rule out cancer!
Missing the family history of colorectal cancer - this patient should have started screening at 40
Not recognizing that BRIGHT red blood can still be from a proximal source in fast transit
Being reassured by the patient's 'young' age - colorectal cancer is increasingly common in <50

evidence.title: ACG Clinical Guideline: Lower GI Bleeding 2023
evidence.cite: Strate LL, et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2023;118:208-231
points: Hemorrhoids are a common cause but malignancy must be excluded
Colonoscopy required to rule out colorectal cancer in patients >45
Risk factors for colorectal cancer increase urgency of evaluation

teaching.keyPoint: Diverticular Bleeding is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Profuse bleeding from rectum, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Assuming hematochezia = LGIB - a brisk UGIB can present with bright red blood per rectum in up to 11% of cases
Not placing NG tube to rule out upper source, especially with hemodynamic instability
Ordering barium enema - it's CONTRAINDICATED in acute LGIB
Not recognizing orthostatic changes as indicator of significant blood loss (>800 mL)

evidence.title: ACG Clinical Guideline: Lower GI Bleeding 2023
evidence.cite: Strate LL, et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2023;118:208-231
points: Diverticular bleeding is the most common cause of acute LGIB
Risk stratification guides timing of colonoscopy
Colonoscopy within 24 hours for hemodynamically stable patients
Hemodynamic resuscitation is priority before endoscopy

reasoning: Over-resuscitating with fluids - increases portal pressure and rebleeding risk

Not involving GI emergently for EGD within 12 hours

Antibiotic prophylaxis with ceftriaxone reduces mortality
Octreotide/terlipressin as pharmacologic therapy
Endoscopic band ligation is first-line endoscopic therapy

She reports that symptoms began 6 days ago and have been progressively worsening. She describes the pain as substernal, pressure-like, radiating to the left arm, and associated with diaphoresis and nausea.

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She quit smoking 3 years ago after a 15-pack-year history; denies alcohol use; works as a homemaker; lives with spouse.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 100.8°F
HR 104 bpm
BP 110/68 mm Hg
RR 18/min
SpO₂ 99%

Physical Examination:
General: anxious-appearing, mildly diaphoretic
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs
Abdomen: Distended, diffusely tender with hypoactive bowel sounds; mild guarding in the epigastric region
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 6.2 × 10³/µL, Hgb 13.0 g/dL, Plt 268 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.2 mEq/L, Cl 98 mEq/L, CO₂ 25 mEq/L, BUN 21 mg/dL, Cr 1.1 mg/dL, Glucose 96 mg/dL

stem: The combination of history, physical examination, and diagnostic studies is most suggestive of which condition?

teaching.keyPoint: Acute Cholecystitis is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Sharp pain in my upper right belly since last night, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Confusing biliary COLIC (self-limited, <6 hours) with CHOLECYSTITIS (prolonged, with fever) - duration and fever are key
Not recognizing the 4 F's: Female, Forty, Fertile (estrogen), Fat - but don't forget males get gallstones too
Missing Charcot's triad (fever + jaundice + RUQ pain) which indicates cholangitis - a more serious condition
Forgetting to check for Murphy's sign - inspiratory arrest with RUQ palpation is highly specific for cholecystitis

evidence.title: Tokyo Guidelines 2018 for Acute Cholecystitis
evidence.cite: Yokoe M, et al. J Hepatobiliary Pancreat Sci. 2018;25:41-54
points: Murphy's sign + fever + elevated WBC suggests diagnosis
Ultrasound is first-line imaging
Early cholecystectomy (within 72 hours) is preferred
Grade severity to guide management approach

teaching.keyPoint: Early Pregnancy with Hyperemesis Gravidarum is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of I've been throwing up for two days and can't keep anything down, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: NOT GETTING A PREGNANCY TEST - the cardinal sin in any reproductive-age woman with nausea/vomiting
Assuming irregular periods means she can't be pregnant
Not recognizing that patients often don't consider pregnancy as a cause of their symptoms
Forgetting that hyperemesis causes metabolic ALKALOSIS (from vomiting H+), not acidosis

vignette:
A 53-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "Fever and abdominal pain." He reports that symptoms began 2 days ago and have been progressively worsening. He describes the pain as substernal, pressure-like, radiating to the left arm, and associated with diaphoresis and nausea.

Past Medical History: hyperlipidemia.

Medications: atorvastatin 40 mg daily.

Allergies: Penicillin (rash).

Social History: He current smoker with a 30-pack-year history; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a nurse; lives alone.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 101.4°F
HR 108 bpm
BP 98/62 mm Hg
RR 22/min
SpO₂ 94%

Physical Examination:
General: fatigued-appearing but cooperative
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs
Abdomen: Distended, diffusely tender with hypoactive bowel sounds; mild guarding in the epigastric region
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 9.5 × 10³/µL, Hgb 13.6 g/dL, Plt 195 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 140 mEq/L, K 4.3 mEq/L, Cl 99 mEq/L, CO₂ 23 mEq/L, BUN 13 mg/dL, Cr 1.0 mg/dL, Glucose 116 mg/dL
LFTs: AST 95 U/L, ALT 144 U/L, Alk Phos 88 U/L, T. Bili 2.7 mg/dL
Lipase 413 U/L (ref 10-140)

Imaging:

stem: Which of the following best explains this patient's presentation?

teaching.keyPoint: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Fever and abdominal pain, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Not performing diagnostic paracentesis in any cirrhotic patient with fever
Waiting for culture results before starting antibiotics
Missing the elevated PMN count as the key diagnostic criterion
Forgetting IV albumin which reduces mortality by preventing hepatorenal syndrome

evidence.title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Evans L, et al. Crit Care Med. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143
points: Septic shock: antimicrobials within 1 hour (Strong)
30 mL/kg crystalloid, but use caution in cirrhosis - increased bleeding risk
Consider albumin for patients receiving large volumes of crystalloids (Weak)
Target MAP ≥65 mmHg with norepinephrine

vignette:
A 28-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "Pain in my lower right belly since yesterday." She reports that symptoms began 2 days ago and have been progressively worsening. She describes the pain as substernal, pressure-like, radiating to the left arm, and associated with diaphoresis and nausea.

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She current smoker with a 30-pack-year history; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a retired teacher; lives alone.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 101.2°F
HR 102 bpm
BP 112/70 mm Hg
RR 18/min
SpO₂ 98%

Physical Examination:
General: alert and oriented, appears ill
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 8.1 × 10³/µL, Hgb 12.7 g/dL, Plt 197 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 138 mEq/L, K 4.1 mEq/L, Cl 101 mEq/L, CO₂ 27 mEq/L, BUN 18 mg/dL, Cr 0.9 mg/dL, Glucose 107 mg/dL
stem: The combination of history, physical examination, and diagnostic studies is most suggestive of which condition?

teaching.keyPoint: Acute Appendicitis is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Pain in my lower right belly since yesterday, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Not asking about pain MIGRATION - periumbilical to RLQ is the most specific historical feature for appendicitis
Forgetting the sequence rule: in appendicitis, PAIN comes first, then nausea/vomiting. If vomiting precedes pain, think gastroenteritis
Assuming patient can't be pregnant because she uses contraception - ALWAYS get a pregnancy test in reproductive-age women
Missing that the patient 'lies still' - this is a key observation distinguishing peritonitis (lies still) from colic (restless)

evidence.title: SAGES/EAES Guidelines for Appendicitis
evidence.cite: Di Saverio S, et al. Surg Endosc. 2020;34:2654-2668

platform.title: Practice Case: Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman

vignette:
A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "Sudden severe pain in my lower right belly." She reports that symptoms began 4 days ago and have been progressively worsening. She describes the pain as substernal, pressure-like, radiating to the left arm, and associated with diaphoresis and nausea.

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: Penicillin (rash).

Social History: She current smoker with a 30-pack-year history; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a construction worker; lives alone.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 99.4°F
HR 108 bpm
BP 118/74 mm Hg
RR 20/min
SpO₂ 99%

Physical Examination:
General: fatigued-appearing but cooperative
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 7.8 × 10³/µL, Hgb 12.4 g/dL, Plt 223 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 141 mEq/L, K 4.3 mEq/L, Cl 102 mEq/L, CO₂ 22 mEq/L, BUN 18 mg/dL, Cr 1.1 mg/dL, Glucose 112 mg/dL
stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which diagnosis is most consistent with the findings?

teaching.keyPoint: Ovarian Torsion is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Sudden severe pain in my lower right belly, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Assuming RLQ pain in women is always appendicitis - must consider ovarian pathology
Not recognizing SUDDEN ONSET severe pain as characteristic of torsion (vs. gradual onset in appendicitis)
Forgetting that nausea/vomiting occurs in torsion due to ovarian nerve stimulation
Not doing pelvic ultrasound when appendix appears normal on CT

vignette:
A 32-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "Terrible headache with nausea and light sensitivity." She reports that symptoms began 6 days ago and have been progressively worsening.

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She quit smoking 10 years ago after a 20-pack-year history; denies alcohol use; works as a office worker; lives alone.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.4°F
HR 78 bpm
BP 118/76 mm Hg
RR 14/min
SpO₂ 99%

Physical Examination:
General: alert and oriented, appears ill
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 8.6 × 10³/µL, Hgb 12.7 g/dL, Plt 274 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 138 mEq/L, K 3.9 mEq/L, Cl 103 mEq/L, CO₂ 27 mEq/L, BUN 13 mg/dL, Cr 0.9 mg/dL, Glucose 93 mg/dL
stem: The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following diagnoses?

teaching.keyPoint: Migraine with Aura is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Terrible headache with nausea and light sensitivity, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Ordering CT scan for every headache - unnecessary radiation and cost for typical migraine
Not asking about aura symptoms - visual changes preceding headache are diagnostic for migraine with aura
Missing the triggers - stress, sleep changes, menstrual cycle are common migraine triggers
Forgetting to ask about similar prior headaches - migraine is typically recurrent

evidence.title: AHS Acute Migraine Treatment Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Ailani J, et al. Headache. 2021;61:1021-1039
points: Triptans remain first-line for acute moderate-severe migraine
NSAIDs effective for mild-moderate attacks
Red flags (SNOOP): Systemic symptoms, Neurologic signs, Onset sudden, Older age, Pattern change
CGRP antagonists (gepants) alternative for triptan failures

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: He quit smoking 10 years ago after a 20-pack-year history; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a accountant; lives alone.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 101.8°F
HR 88 bpm
BP 112/68 mm Hg
RR 16/min
SpO₂ 99%

Physical Examination:
General: fatigued-appearing but cooperative
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 8.3 × 10³/µL, Hgb 12.9 g/dL, Plt 287 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 136 mEq/L, K 4.0 mEq/L, Cl 100 mEq/L, CO₂ 22 mEq/L, BUN 14 mg/dL, Cr 1.2 mg/dL, Glucose 109 mg/dL
stem: Which of the following best explains this patient's presentation?

teaching.keyPoint: Streptococcal Pharyngitis (Strep Throat) is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Really bad sore throat for 2 days, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Using Centor criteria incorrectly - they guide testing, not empiric treatment
Prescribing antibiotics without testing - rapid strep or culture should guide therapy
Missing the tonsillar exudates on exam - must look carefully with good lighting
Forgetting to check for splenomegaly in suspected mono - contact sports restriction needed

evidence.title: IDSA Group A Streptococcal Pharyngitis Guidelines 2012

points: Use Centor/McIsaac criteria for testing decisions
Rapid antigen test first; backup throat culture if negative in children
Penicillin or amoxicillin first-line for confirmed GAS
10 days of treatment to prevent rheumatic fever

vignette:
A 26-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "Pain in my right lower belly." She reports that symptoms began 6 days ago and have been progressively worsening. She describes the pain as substernal, pressure-like, radiating to the left arm, and associated with diaphoresis and nausea.

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She quit smoking 3 years ago after a 15-pack-year history; reports drinking 1-2 beers daily; works as a retired teacher; lives with spouse.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.8°F
HR 108 bpm
BP 102/68 mm Hg
RR 18/min
SpO₂ 99%

Physical Examination:
General: appears uncomfortable but in no acute distress
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 8.8 × 10³/µL, Hgb 12.5 g/dL, Plt 181 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 137 mEq/L, K 3.8 mEq/L, Cl 99 mEq/L, CO₂ 25 mEq/L, BUN 17 mg/dL, Cr 1.0 mg/dL, Glucose 110 mg/dL
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Ectopic Pregnancy is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Pain in my right lower belly, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: NOT GETTING A PREGNANCY TEST - this is the cardinal error. Pregnancy test in ALL reproductive-age women with abdominal pain, no exceptions
Being reassured by 'she uses contraception' - no contraception is 100% effective
Missing Kehr's sign (shoulder pain) - referred pain from diaphragmatic irritation by blood indicates hemoperitoneum
Waiting for type-specific blood before surgery - in unstable patient, give O-negative and go to OR

vignette:
A 19-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "Nausea, vomiting, and confusion." She reports that symptoms began 5 days ago and have been progressively worsening.

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She never smoked; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a nurse; lives with spouse.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 99.4°F
HR 118 bpm
BP 102/58 mm Hg
RR 28/min
SpO₂ 99%

Physical Examination:
General: anxious-appearing, mildly diaphoretic
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 7.4 × 10³/µL, Hgb 12.8 g/dL, Plt 200 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 136 mEq/L, K 4.3 mEq/L, Cl 100 mEq/L, CO₂ 23 mEq/L, BUN 21 mg/dL, Cr 0.8 mg/dL, Glucose 90 mg/dL
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Nausea, vomiting, and confusion, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Giving bicarbonate (rarely indicated, can cause paradoxical CNS acidosis)
Not looking for the trigger (infection, MI, medication non-compliance)
Stopping insulin when glucose normalizes (need to close anion gap first)
Not checking potassium before insulin (can cause fatal hypokalemia)

evidence.title: ADA Standards of Care - Diabetes in Hospital
evidence.cite: ADA Professional Practice Committee. Diabetes Care. 2024;47(Suppl 1):S295-S306
points: DKA defined by glucose >250, pH <7.3, bicarb <18, anion gap >10, ketones
Fluid resuscitation: NS 15-20 mL/kg/hr initially
Insulin infusion 0.1 units/kg/hr after initial fluids

Starting beta-blocker acutely in a beta-blocker naive patient

Temp 98.6°F

SpO₂ 98%

stem: The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following diagnoses?

teaching.keyPoint: Hypertension is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Annual physical - wife made me come, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Not asking about alcohol in enough detail - patients minimize use
Accepting 'social drinker' without quantifying

evidence.title: ASAM Clinical Practice Guideline on Alcohol Withdrawal Management
evidence.cite: ASAM. J Addict Med. 2020;14(3S):1-72
points: ASSESSMENT: Screen ALL patients for alcohol use; assess withdrawal risk with PAWSS (Prediction of Alcohol Withdrawal Severity Scale) for at-risk patients
MILD-MODERATE WITHDRAWAL (CIWA-Ar <15): Symptom-triggered therapy preferred over fixed-dose; reduces medication use and treatment duration
SEVERE WITHDRAWAL (CIWA-Ar ≥15): May require fixed-dosing or front-loading approach with close monitoring
BENZODIAZEPINES: First-line for AWS; no single agent proven superior; long-acting (diazepam, chlordiazepoxide) vs short-acting (lorazepam) based on liver function

platform.title: Practice Case: Nausea and Abdominal Pain

vignette:
A 45-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "Severe pain under my right ribs after eating." She reports that symptoms began 6 days ago and have been progressively worsening. She describes the pain as substernal, pressure-like, radiating to the left arm, and associated with diaphoresis and nausea.

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She current smoker with a 30-pack-year history; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a truck driver; lives alone.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 101.2°F
HR 96 bpm
BP 142/88 mm Hg
RR 18/min
SpO₂ 98%

Physical Examination:
General: fatigued-appearing but cooperative
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 8.0 × 10³/µL, Hgb 12.2 g/dL, Plt 218 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 136 mEq/L, K 3.6 mEq/L, Cl 99 mEq/L, CO₂ 22 mEq/L, BUN 19 mg/dL, Cr 0.8 mg/dL, Glucose 95 mg/dL
stem: The combination of history, physical examination, and diagnostic studies is most suggestive of which condition?

teaching.keyPoint: Acute Cholecystitis is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Severe pain under my right ribs after eating, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Confusing biliary colic with cholecystitis - colic is episodic and self-limited, cholecystitis has continuous pain and fever
Missing the 'fat, fertile, forty, female' risk factors while being careful not to stereotype
Not recognizing Murphy's sign when it's positive
Forgetting that cholecystitis can occur without gallstones (acalculous cholecystitis)

evidence.title: Tokyo Guidelines 2018 for Acute Cholecystitis
evidence.cite: Yokoe M, et al. J Hepatobiliary Pancreat Sci. 2018;25:41-54
points: Murphy's sign + fever + elevated WBC suggests diagnosis
Ultrasound is first-line imaging
Early cholecystectomy (within 72 hours) is preferred
Grade severity to guide management approach

platform.title: Practice Case: Right Lower Quadrant Pain

vignette:
A 68-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "Pre-operative clearance for total knee replacement." He reports that symptoms began 2 days ago and have been progressively worsening.

Past Medical History: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, osteoarthritis, benign prostatic hyperplasia.

Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily.

Allergies: Penicillin (rash).

Social History: He quit smoking 10 years ago after a 20-pack-year history; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a truck driver; lives with spouse.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.4°F
HR 72 bpm
BP 148/88 mm Hg
RR 14/min
SpO₂ 96%

Physical Examination:
General: fatigued-appearing but cooperative
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 8.6 × 10³/µL, Hgb 13.8 g/dL, Plt 235 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 141 mEq/L, K 3.9 mEq/L, Cl 99 mEq/L, CO₂ 23 mEq/L, BUN 17 mg/dL, Cr 1.2 mg/dL, Glucose 107 mg/dL
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Intermediate Cardiac Risk - Proceed with Surgery

teaching.keyPoint: Intermediate Cardiac Risk - Proceed with Surgery is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Pre-operative clearance for total knee replacement, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Ordering unnecessary cardiac testing in low-risk patients - delays surgery without benefit
Not assessing functional capacity - METs are key to risk stratification
Missing OSA screening - STOP-BANG should be routine
Not knowing which medications to hold vs continue perioperatively

vignette:
A 72-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "Severe stomach pain and vomiting for 2 days." She reports that symptoms began 2 days ago and have been progressively worsening. She describes the pain as substernal, pressure-like, radiating to the left arm, and associated with diaphoresis and nausea.

Past Medical History: hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, osteoarthritis, coronary artery disease.

Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, empagliflozin 10 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg daily.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She never smoked; denies alcohol use; works as a retired teacher; lives with adult children.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 99.8°F
HR 108 bpm
BP 102/68 mm Hg
RR 22/min
SpO₂ 96%

Physical Examination:
General: appears uncomfortable but in no acute distress
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 9.6 × 10³/µL, Hgb 12.0 g/dL, Plt 218 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 140 mEq/L, K 3.8 mEq/L, Cl 98 mEq/L, CO₂ 26 mEq/L, BUN 18 mg/dL, Cr 1.0 mg/dL, Glucose 123 mg/dL
stem: The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following diagnoses?

teaching.keyPoint: Adhesive Small Bowel Obstruction is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Severe stomach pain and vomiting for 2 days, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Missing the surgical history - prior abdominal surgery is #1 cause of SBO in developed countries
Assuming obstipation means complete obstruction - partial SBO can still pass some gas/stool initially
Not recognizing signs of strangulation (fever, tachycardia, localized tenderness, leukocytosis)
Relying on labs - lactate and WBC can be normal early in strangulation

evidence.title: SAGES Guidelines for SBO Management
evidence.cite: Maung AA, et al. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2012;73:S362-S369
points: CT scan is imaging modality of choice
NGT decompression and IV fluids for initial management
Signs of strangulation require emergent surgery
Water-soluble contrast can be therapeutic and diagnostic

teaching.keyPoint: Catheter-Associated UTI is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Fever on post-operative day 3, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.

evidence.title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Evans L, et al. Crit Care Med. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143
points: Continuously reevaluate for alternative diagnoses in suspected sepsis (Best Practice)
If septic shock develops: antimicrobials within 1 hour (Strong)
Prompt removal of intravascular devices that are possible infection source (Best Practice)
Source control - address surgical site infection or abscess

vignette:
A 58-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "Sudden severe stomach pain like I was stabbed." He reports that symptoms began 5 days ago and have been progressively worsening. He describes the pain as substernal, pressure-like, radiating to the left arm, and associated with diaphoresis and nausea.

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: He quit smoking 10 years ago after a 20-pack-year history; denies alcohol use; works as a construction worker; lives alone.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 101.4°F
HR 122 bpm
BP 92/58 mm Hg
RR 26/min
SpO₂ 94%

Physical Examination:
General: alert and oriented, appears ill
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 7.5 × 10³/µL, Hgb 13.1 g/dL, Plt 204 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.2 mEq/L, Cl 103 mEq/L, CO₂ 23 mEq/L, BUN 17 mg/dL, Cr 1.0 mg/dL, Glucose 106 mg/dL
stem: The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following diagnoses?

teaching.keyPoint: Perforated Peptic Ulcer is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Sudden severe stomach pain like I was stabbed, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Delaying surgery for 'optimization' - source control is priority
Missing free air on supine X-ray - need upright or CT
Not recognizing the 'rigid abdomen' - classic peritonitis sign
Assuming patient with prior pain is having 'another episode'

evidence.title: WSES Guidelines for Management of Intra-Abdominal Infections 2017
evidence.cite: Sartelli M, et al. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:29
points: Perforated viscus requires emergent surgical intervention
Source control is priority - cannot treat with antibiotics alone
CT with oral/IV contrast is imaging of choice
Free air on imaging is diagnostic but absent in 20% of perforations

platform.title: Practice Case: Severe Epigastric Pain Radiating to Back

vignette:
A 75-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "Painful lump in my groin and I've been vomiting all day." She reports that symptoms began 5 days ago and have been progressively worsening. She describes the pain as substernal, pressure-like, radiating to the left arm, and associated with diaphoresis and nausea.

Past Medical History: hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, coronary artery disease.

Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, aspirin 81 mg daily.

Allergies: Penicillin (rash).

Social History: She quit smoking 3 years ago after a 15-pack-year history; denies alcohol use; works as a construction worker; lives with adult children.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 100.8°F
HR 108 bpm
BP 108/72 mm Hg
RR 20/min
SpO₂ 96%

Physical Examination:
General: appears uncomfortable but in no acute distress
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 8.7 × 10³/µL, Hgb 12.1 g/dL, Plt 309 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.0 mEq/L, Cl 101 mEq/L, CO₂ 24 mEq/L, BUN 16 mg/dL, Cr 1.2 mg/dL, Glucose 114 mg/dL
stem: The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following diagnoses?

teaching.keyPoint: Strangulated Femoral Hernia is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Painful lump in my groin and I've been vomiting all day, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Attempting aggressive manual reduction of a strangulated hernia - risk of reducing dead bowel
Mistaking femoral hernia for inguinal - femoral is BELOW inguinal ligament
Not recognizing obstruction symptoms as hernia-related
Delaying surgery while 'optimizing' patient - strangulated bowel doesn't wait

evidence.title: European Hernia Society Guidelines on Groin Hernia 2018
evidence.cite: HerniaSurge Group. Hernia. 2018;22(1):1-165
points: Incarcerated hernia: cannot be reduced (surgical urgency)
Strangulated hernia: compromised blood supply (surgical emergency)
Signs of strangulation: severe pain, tenderness, erythema, fever, obstruction symptoms
Emergency surgery within 4-6 hours if strangulation suspected

vignette:
A 78-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "Terrible belly pain - worst of my life - but doctor says my belly is soft." She reports that symptoms began 4 days ago and have been progressively worsening. She describes the pain as substernal, pressure-like, radiating to the left arm, and associated with diaphoresis and nausea.

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She current smoker with a 30-pack-year history; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a truck driver; lives with adult children.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.8°F
HR 112 bpm
BP 148/88 mm Hg
RR 22/min
SpO₂ 95%

Physical Examination:
General: alert and oriented, appears ill
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 7.3 × 10³/µL, Hgb 12.4 g/dL, Plt 297 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 140 mEq/L, K 4.1 mEq/L, Cl 98 mEq/L, CO₂ 26 mEq/L, BUN 20 mg/dL, Cr 1.0 mg/dL, Glucose 97 mg/dL
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Acute Mesenteric Ischemia (SMA Embolism) is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Terrible belly pain - worst of my life - but doctor says my belly is soft, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Reassured by 'soft abdomen' - classic early finding is pain OUT OF PROPORTION to exam
Waiting for lactate to rise - lactate is LATE finding when bowel is already dead
Not considering in AF patient with abdominal pain - cardiac emboli cause 50% of cases
Ordering CT without angiography phase - need CTA to see vessels

evidence.title: ACG Clinical Guideline: Mesenteric Ischemia 2020
evidence.cite: Defined TL, et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2020;115(8):1323-1339
points: Pain out of proportion to physical exam is classic early finding
CT angiography is imaging of choice
Arterial: SMA embolism (50%), SMA thrombosis (25%), NOMI (20%)
Venous: Mesenteric vein thrombosis (5-15%)

platform.title: Practice Case: Belly Pain with Fever

vignette:
A 62-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "Terrible pain in my private area with swelling and fever." He reports that symptoms began 2 days ago and have been progressively worsening. He describes the pain as substernal, pressure-like, radiating to the left arm, and associated with diaphoresis and nausea.

Past Medical History: hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, osteoarthritis.

Medications: losartan 50 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: He never smoked; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a homemaker; lives alone.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 103.8°F
HR 128 bpm
BP 88/52 mm Hg
RR 26/min
SpO₂ 92%

Physical Examination:
General: alert and oriented, appears ill
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 8.5 × 10³/µL, Hgb 12.5 g/dL, Plt 214 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 139 mEq/L, K 3.6 mEq/L, Cl 102 mEq/L, CO₂ 24 mEq/L, BUN 19 mg/dL, Cr 1.1 mg/dL, Glucose 101 mg/dL
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Fournier's Gangrene is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Terrible pain in my private area with swelling and fever, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: Mistaking for simple cellulitis or abscess - NSTI progresses much faster
Waiting for imaging to confirm diagnosis - delays lifesaving surgery
Inadequate debridement - must remove ALL necrotic tissue
Not recognizing pain out of proportion to visible findings - hallmark of NSTI

evidence.title: WSES/GAIS Guidelines for Necrotizing Soft Tissue Infections 2018
evidence.cite: Sartelli M, et al. World J Emerg Surg. 2018;13:50
points: Fournier's gangrene is necrotizing fasciitis of perineum/genitalia
Surgical emergency - mortality 20-40% even with treatment
Risk factors: Diabetes, immunosuppression, obesity, alcohol
Polymicrobial infection (mixed aerobic and anaerobic)

platform.title: Practice Case: Recurrent Swelling Without Hives

vignette:
A 28-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of "I feel like I can't breathe and my heart is racing." She reports that symptoms began 4 days ago and have been progressively worsening. She describes a productive cough with yellow-green sputum, associated fevers and chills, and increasing shortness of breath with exertion.

Past Medical History: no significant past medical history.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: She quit smoking 10 years ago after a 20-pack-year history; drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2-3 drinks per week; works as a truck driver; lives with spouse.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 62; mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Vital Signs
Temp 99.0°F
HR 118 bpm
BP 128/82 mm Hg
RR 24/min
SpO₂ 94%

Physical Examination:
General: fatigued-appearing but cooperative
HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, no pharyngeal erythema or exudate
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 6.9 × 10³/µL, Hgb 13.2 g/dL, Plt 270 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 141 mEq/L, K 3.8 mEq/L, Cl 99 mEq/L, CO₂ 27 mEq/L, BUN 18 mg/dL, Cr 1.0 mg/dL, Glucose 90 mg/dL
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Anxiety/Panic Attack is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of I feel like I can't breathe and my heart is racing, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: ⚠️ THIS IS A TRAP CASE - the 'obvious' diagnosis (anxiety) is WRONG
Young, healthy-appearing woman with 'anxiety symptoms' - easy to dismiss as psychiatric
Missing the subtle hypoxia (SpO2 94%) - anxiety doesn't cause hypoxia!
Not asking about OCP use - estrogen is a major VTE risk factor

evidence.title: 2019 ESC Guidelines for Acute Pulmonary Embolism
evidence.cite: Konstantinides SV, et al. Eur Heart J. 2020;41:543-603
points: High-risk PE presents with hemodynamic instability - shock or sustained hypotension
Wells score and Geneva score help assess clinical probability
D-dimer has high NPV in low-probability patients
CTPA is the diagnostic standard for PE

General: fatigued-appearing but cooperative

Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Laboratory Studies:
CBC: WBC 7.3 × 10³/µL, Hgb 14.3 g/dL, Plt 234 × 10³/µL
BMP: Na 137 mEq/L, K 4.0 mEq/L, Cl 100 mEq/L, CO₂ 26 mEq/L, BUN 19 mg/dL, Cr 0.8 mg/dL, Glucose 129 mg/dL
stem: The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following diagnoses?

teaching.keyPoint: GERD is the most likely diagnosis. The combination of Bad heartburn that won't go away, the vital sign abnormalities, physical examination findings, and diagnostic studies point to this diagnosis.
reasoning: ⚠️ THIS IS A TRAP CASE - the 'obvious' diagnosis (GERD) is WRONG
Women present atypically with ACS more often than men - 'heartburn' is a common presentation
Being fooled by relief with antacids - nitroglycerin relief is also nonspecific, and so is antacid relief
Missing bradycardia - HR 58 in a patient with 'heartburn' should raise suspicion for inferior MI

distractors.Viral syndrome: While viral syndrome may present with some overlapping features, the specific constellation of findings in this case—including the laboratory values and imaging—makes this diagnosis less likely.
distractors.Bacterial meningitis: While bacterial meningitis may present with some overlapping features, the specific constellation of findings in this case—including the laboratory values and imaging—makes this diagnosis less likely.
distractors.Sepsis secondary to pneumonia: While sepsis secondary to pneumonia may present with some overlapping features, the specific constellation of findings in this case—including the laboratory values and imaging—makes this diagnosis less likely.
distractors.Intra-abdominal abscess: While intra-abdominal abscess may present with some overlapping features, the specific constellation of findings in this case—including the laboratory values and imaging—makes this diagnosis less likely.

vignette:
A 72-year-old woman with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and chronic kidney disease stage 3 presents to the emergency department with confusion, fever, and dysuria for 2 days. Her daughter reports she has been increasingly lethargic over the past 24 hours.

Past Medical History: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 8.2%), hypertension, chronic kidney disease stage 3 (baseline creatinine 1.6 mg/dL), recurrent urinary tract infections.

Medications: Metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily.

Allergies: Sulfonamides (anaphylaxis).

Social History: Lives in assisted living facility; former smoker (quit 15 years ago); no alcohol use.

Vital Signs
Temp 102.8°F (39.3°C)
HR 118 bpm
BP 88/52 mm Hg
RR 24/min
SpO₂ 94% on room air

Physical Examination:
General: Ill-appearing, confused, oriented to person only
Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally
Abdomen: Soft, mild suprapubic tenderness, no costovertebral angle tenderness
Skin: Warm, dry, no rashes
Extremities: No edema, capillary refill 4 seconds

Laboratory Studies:
WBC: 18.2 × 10³/µL with 15% bands
Hemoglobin: 11.8 g/dL, Platelets: 142 × 10³/µL
Na: 134 mEq/L, K: 5.1 mEq/L, Cl: 98 mEq/L, CO₂: 18 mEq/L
BUN: 42 mg/dL, Creatinine: 2.8 mg/dL (baseline 1.6)
Glucose: 286 mg/dL
Lactate: 4.2 mmol/L
Urinalysis: Large leukocyte esterase, positive nitrites, >100 WBC/hpf, many bacteria
stem: In addition to obtaining blood cultures and initiating intravenous fluid resuscitation, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

teaching.keyPoint: Cefepime 2g IV is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic for this patient with urosepsis. She has septic shock (hypotension, elevated lactate, evidence of organ dysfunction) from a urinary source. Cefepime provides appropriate gram-negative coverage including Pseudomonas, which is important in healthcare-associated infections. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is contraindicated due to her sulfa allergy. Fluoroquinolones have increasing resistance and should not be first-line for severe infections. The combination of vancomycin plus piperacillin-tazobactam is broader than necessary for a clear urinary source.
reasoning: Sulfa allergy (anaphylaxis) contraindicates TMP-SMX
Healthcare facility residence increases Pseudomonas risk
Septic shock requires prompt broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour
Source control and monitoring for response are essential

evidence.title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Evans L, et al. Crit Care Med. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143
points: Administer antibiotics within 1 hour for septic shock (strong recommendation)
Empiric broad-spectrum therapy should cover likely pathogens
Reassess antimicrobial therapy daily for de-escalation

distractors.Ceftriaxone 1g IV and azithromycin 500mg: This regimen is appropriate for community-acquired pneumonia but does not provide optimal coverage for complicated urinary tract infections, particularly in a healthcare-associated setting.
distractors.Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole IV: This is contraindicated in this patient due to her documented anaphylaxis to sulfonamides.
distractors.Ciprofloxacin 400mg IV: While fluoroquinolones cover urinary pathogens, increasing resistance rates and the severity of this patient's presentation make this a suboptimal choice for empiric therapy.
distractors.Vancomycin 1g IV and piperacillin-tazoba: This combination provides broader coverage than necessary for a clear urinary source without evidence of gram-positive or resistant organisms.

platform.title: Practice Case: Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman

vignette:
A 58-year-old man with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and type 2 diabetes mellitus presents to the emergency department with chest pain that began 3 hours ago. He describes the pain as a pressure-like sensation in the center of his chest, radiating to his left arm and jaw, associated with diaphoresis and nausea. The pain is not relieved by rest or antacids.

Past Medical History: Hypertension (10 years), hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes mellitus, former smoker (quit 2 years ago, 40 pack-year history).

Medications: Lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, aspirin 81 mg daily.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Family History: Father had myocardial infarction at age 52; mother has heart failure.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.4°F
HR 92 bpm
BP 158/94 mm Hg
RR 18/min
SpO₂ 97% on room air

Physical Examination:
General: Anxious-appearing, mildly diaphoretic
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, S4 gallop present, no murmurs or rubs
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender
Extremities: No edema, peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally

Initial Studies:
ECG: Sinus rhythm at 92 bpm, 2mm ST depressions in leads V3-V6 and leads I, aVL; no ST elevations
Troponin I: 0.08 ng/mL (reference <0.04 ng/mL) - drawn on arrival

The patient receives aspirin 325 mg (chewed), sublingual nitroglycerin with partial relief, and is started on a heparin infusion.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient?

options.text: Serial troponins and repeat ECG in 3-6 hours

teaching.keyPoint: Invasive coronary angiography is indicated for this patient with NSTEMI (positive troponin, ST depressions, ongoing symptoms). Current guidelines recommend an early invasive strategy (within 24 hours) for high-risk NSTE-ACS patients. This patient has multiple high-risk features: dynamic ECG changes, positive troponin, ongoing symptoms despite initial therapy, diabetes, and high TIMI/GRACE risk score. Serial troponins are useful when the diagnosis is uncertain, but this patient already has a confirmed NSTEMI. Non-invasive testing is not appropriate in the acute setting with confirmed ACS.
reasoning: Positive troponin with dynamic ECG changes confirms NSTEMI
High-risk features warrant early invasive strategy
Ongoing symptoms despite medical therapy increase urgency
Non-invasive testing is inappropriate with confirmed ACS

evidence.title: ACC/AHA NSTE-ACS Guidelines 2014 (Updated 2021)
evidence.cite: Amsterdam EA, et al. Circulation. 2014;130:e344-e426
points: Early invasive strategy (within 24 hours) for high-risk NSTE-ACS
High-risk features: positive troponin, ST changes, refractory symptoms, hemodynamic instability
Dual antiplatelet therapy and anticoagulation should precede angiography

distractors.CT coronary angiography: CT coronary angiography is useful for intermediate-risk patients when the diagnosis is uncertain. This patient has confirmed NSTEMI and requires invasive evaluation.
distractors.Stress echocardiography: Stress testing is contraindicated in patients with confirmed acute coronary syndrome and should not be performed in the acute setting.
distractors.Serial troponins and repeat ECG in 3-6 h: While serial troponins are standard, this patient already has diagnostic criteria for NSTEMI. Further observation delays definitive therapy.
distractors.Cardiac MRI: Cardiac MRI may be useful for myocarditis or stress cardiomyopathy evaluation but is not the appropriate next step in confirmed NSTEMI.

platform.title: Practice Case: Increased Thirst and Urination

vignette:
A 67-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by EMS after her husband found her with right-sided weakness and difficulty speaking this morning. He reports she was normal when she went to bed at 11:00 PM last night. He discovered her symptoms when she woke at 6:30 AM. The current time is 7:15 AM.

Past Medical History: Hypertension, atrial fibrillation (on warfarin), type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia.

Medications: Warfarin 5 mg daily, metoprolol 50 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 80 mg daily.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.2°F
HR 82 bpm (irregularly irregular)
BP 178/96 mm Hg
RR 16/min
SpO₂ 98% on room air

Physical Examination:
General: Alert but appears frustrated, speaking with difficulty
Neurological: Expressive aphasia with intact comprehension; right facial droop (lower face); right hemiparesis (arm 2/5, leg 3/5); right-sided sensory neglect; gaze preference to the left
NIHSS Score: 14

Laboratory Studies:
Point-of-care glucose: 142 mg/dL
INR: 1.8

Imaging:
CT Head without contrast (obtained within 10 minutes of arrival): No hemorrhage, no early ischemic changes, no mass effect. ASPECTS score 9.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Obtain CT angiography of head and neck and consider mechanical thrombectomy
Administer 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate followed by IV alteplase
Start aspirin 325 mg and admit to stroke unit
Obtain MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging

teaching.keyPoint: CT angiography and consideration of mechanical thrombectomy is the most appropriate next step. This patient presents with a wake-up stroke with unknown time of onset (last known well >7.5 hours ago), making IV thrombolysis contraindicated within the standard time window. However, she has a significant deficit (NIHSS 14) suggesting large vessel occlusion. Current guidelines support mechanical thrombectomy up to 24 hours from last known well in select patients with favorable imaging (DAWN/DEFUSE-3 criteria). Her elevated INR (1.8) on warfarin also creates additional bleeding risk with systemic thrombolysis. CTA will identify if there is a large vessel occlusion amenable to intervention.
reasoning: Wake-up stroke with last known well >4.5 hours precludes standard IV tPA
INR 1.8 increases hemorrhagic risk with systemic thrombolysis
NIHSS 14 suggests large vessel occlusion
Thrombectomy may be offered up to 24 hours based on perfusion imaging

evidence.title: AHA/ASA Acute Ischemic Stroke Guidelines 2019
evidence.cite: Powers WJ, et al. Stroke. 2019;50:e344-e418
points: Mechanical thrombectomy recommended for LVO within 6 hours (Class I)
Thrombectomy may be considered 6-24 hours in select patients (DAWN/DEFUSE-3 criteria)
IV alteplase within 4.5 hours of symptom onset for eligible patients

distractors.Administer IV alteplase: IV alteplase is contraindicated in this patient due to unknown time of onset (wake-up stroke, last known well >7.5 hours ago) and elevated INR (1.8) from warfarin therapy.
distractors.Administer 4-factor prothrombin complex: While PCC could reverse warfarin, the unknown time window still makes IV thrombolysis inappropriate. Thrombectomy does not require INR reversal.
distractors.Start aspirin 325 mg and admit to stroke: While aspirin is part of secondary prevention, initiating it before ruling out a thrombectomy-eligible large vessel occlusion would be premature and delays potentially beneficial intervention.
distractors.Obtain MRI brain with diffusion-weighted: MRI could help with patient selection, but CTA to identify large vessel occlusion is the priority to determine thrombectomy eligibility. MRI should not delay intervention.

platform.title: Practice Case: Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman

vignette:
A 45-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of shortness of breath and right-sided pleuritic chest pain that started 4 hours ago. She reports the symptoms began while she was sitting at her desk at work. She denies cough, fever, hemoptysis, or leg swelling.

Past Medical History: Obesity (BMI 34), oral contraceptive use for menorrhagia, recent 6-hour car trip 1 week ago.

Medications: Combined oral contraceptive pill.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: Works as a lawyer; denies tobacco, alcohol, or drug use.

Family History: Mother had "blood clots" at age 50; brother had DVT at age 38.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.6°F
HR 108 bpm
BP 128/78 mm Hg
RR 22/min
SpO₂ 92% on room air

Physical Examination:
General: Anxious, mild respiratory distress
Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, prominent P2, no murmurs
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes or crackles
Extremities: No lower extremity edema, no calf tenderness or asymmetry
Skin: No rashes

Laboratory Studies:
WBC: 9.8 × 10³/µL, Hemoglobin: 13.2 g/dL, Platelets: 245 × 10³/µL
BMP: Within normal limits
D-dimer: 2.8 µg/mL FEU (reference <0.5 µg/mL FEU)
Troponin I: 0.06 ng/mL (reference <0.04 ng/mL)
BNP: 180 pg/mL

ECG: Sinus tachycardia at 108 bpm, S1Q3T3 pattern, T-wave inversions in V1-V3.

Chest X-ray: No consolidation, no pleural effusion, normal cardiac silhouette, subtle oligemia in the right lower lung field.
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

teaching.keyPoint: Pulmonary embolism is the most likely diagnosis. This patient has multiple risk factors (obesity, OCP use, recent prolonged travel, strong family history suggesting possible hereditary thrombophilia), presenting with sudden onset dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain. The clinical findings are highly suggestive: tachycardia, hypoxemia, clear lungs, prominent P2 (suggesting pulmonary hypertension), significantly elevated D-dimer, mildly elevated troponin and BNP (indicating right heart strain), classic S1Q3T3 pattern with right precordial T-wave inversions on ECG, and Westermark sign (oligemia) on chest X-ray. CT pulmonary angiography should be obtained to confirm.
reasoning: Wells Score: >4 (PE likely) - tachycardia, recent immobilization, clinical suspicion
Multiple VTE risk factors: OCP, recent travel, family history, obesity
Classic ECG findings: S1Q3T3, RV strain pattern
Elevated troponin and BNP indicate right ventricular strain

evidence.title: ESC Guidelines on Acute Pulmonary Embolism 2019
evidence.cite: Konstantinides SV, et al. Eur Heart J. 2020;41:543-603
points: D-dimer should not be used to rule out PE in high-probability patients
CT pulmonary angiography is the gold standard for diagnosis
Risk stratification using PESI/sPESI guides management
Elevated troponin and BNP indicate intermediate-high risk

distractors.Acute pericarditis: Pericarditis typically presents with positional chest pain (worse supine, improved leaning forward), friction rub, and diffuse ST elevations on ECG. This patient lacks these features.
distractors.Community-acquired pneumonia: Pneumonia typically presents with cough, fever, and focal findings on lung examination and chest X-ray. This patient has clear lungs and no consolidation.
distractors.Acute coronary syndrome: While the mildly elevated troponin could suggest ACS, the clinical presentation (sudden pleuritic pain, clear lungs, S1Q3T3 pattern) and risk factors strongly favor PE.
distractors.Spontaneous pneumothorax: Pneumothorax would show absent breath sounds on the affected side and radiographic findings of collapsed lung, neither of which is present.

platform.title: Practice Case: Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman

vignette:
A 28-year-old previously healthy man presents to the emergency department with abdominal pain for 18 hours. The pain began periumbilically and has now localized to the right lower quadrant. He reports anorexia, one episode of non-bloody vomiting, and low-grade fever. He denies diarrhea or urinary symptoms.

Past Medical History: None.

Medications: None.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Social History: College student; denies tobacco, occasional alcohol use; sexually active with one female partner.

Vital Signs
Temp 100.8°F (38.2°C)
HR 96 bpm
BP 124/78 mm Hg
RR 16/min
SpO₂ 99% on room air

Physical Examination:
General: Uncomfortable, lying still, reluctant to move
Abdomen: Soft, tender in the right lower quadrant with involuntary guarding; positive McBurney point tenderness; positive Rovsing sign (RLQ pain with palpation of LLQ); positive psoas sign; no rebound tenderness; bowel sounds hypoactive
Rectal: Normal tone, no masses, mild tenderness on the right

Laboratory Studies:
WBC: 14.2 × 10³/µL with 82% neutrophils, 8% bands
Hemoglobin: 14.8 g/dL, Platelets: 268 × 10³/µL
BMP: Within normal limits
Urinalysis: Trace blood, otherwise negative
Lipase: 42 U/L (reference 10-140 U/L)

CT Abdomen/Pelvis with IV Contrast:
Dilated appendix measuring 11 mm in diameter with periappendiceal fat stranding. Appendicolith visualized at the base. No abscess or free perforation. Small amount of free fluid in the pelvis.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate management for this patient?

options.text: IV antibiotics alone with close outpatient follow-up
IV antibiotics and percutaneous abscess drainage

teaching.keyPoint: Appendectomy within 24 hours is the standard of care for uncomplicated acute appendicitis. This patient has classic signs and symptoms (migration of pain, anorexia, fever, RLQ tenderness with guarding, positive McBurney, Rovsing, and psoas signs) with CT confirmation. The imaging shows uncomplicated appendicitis (dilated appendix with inflammation but no abscess or perforation). Current evidence supports appendectomy within 24 hours of diagnosis rather than emergent surgery within hours, as outcomes are similar. Antibiotics alone may be considered in select patients but remain controversial and surgical management remains the definitive treatment.
reasoning: Classic clinical presentation with CT confirmation of appendicitis
Uncomplicated appendicitis (no abscess, no perforation)
Appendectomy within 24 hours is standard of care
Emergent surgery (within 2 hours) not required for uncomplicated cases

evidence.title: SAGES Guidelines on Acute Appendicitis 2021
evidence.cite: Korndorffer JR, et al. Surg Endosc. 2021;35:4185-4208
points: Laparoscopic appendectomy is the gold standard
Surgery within 24 hours recommended for uncomplicated appendicitis
Antibiotics alone may be considered but has higher failure/recurrence rates
Perioperative antibiotics should be administered

distractors.IV antibiotics alone with close outpatie: While antibiotics-first strategy is being studied, it has higher rates of recurrence and treatment failure. Surgical management remains the standard for uncomplicated appendicitis in most guidelines.
distractors.IV antibiotics and percutaneous abscess: This approach is appropriate for complicated appendicitis with abscess formation, but imaging shows uncomplicated appendicitis without abscess.
distractors.Observation with serial abdominal examin: The diagnosis is established by CT imaging and clinical findings. Observation delays definitive treatment and risks progression to perforation.
distractors.Emergent open appendectomy within 2 hour: Evidence shows that outcomes are similar whether appendectomy is performed within 2 hours or within 24 hours for uncomplicated cases. Additionally, laparoscopic approach is preferred over open when feasible.

platform.title: Practice Case: Nausea and Abdominal Pain

vignette:
A 62-year-old man with hypertension and type 2 diabetes presents to the emergency department with crushing substernal chest pain radiating to his left arm for the past 45 minutes. He appears diaphoretic and anxious.

Past Medical History: Hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia.

Medications: Lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Vital Signs
BP 148/92 mm Hg
HR 98 bpm
RR 20/min
SpO₂ 96% on RA

Physical Examination: Diaphoretic, S4 gallop, lungs clear, no peripheral edema.

ECG: ST elevations of 3 mm in leads V1-V4 with reciprocal ST depressions in leads II, III, and aVF.

Laboratory: Initial troponin I 0.02 ng/mL (reference <0.04).

Aspirin 325 mg has been administered. The nearest PCI-capable facility is 20 minutes away by ambulance.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Transfer for primary percutaneous coronary intervention
Obtain serial troponins every 3 hours before intervention
Start heparin infusion and admit to CCU for monitoring
Perform stress testing to confirm ischemia

teaching.keyPoint: Primary PCI is preferred for STEMI when door-to-balloon time can be achieved within 90 minutes (or 120 minutes for transfer). This patient has clear STEMI with anterior ST elevations. With PCI available within 20 minutes transport, primary PCI is superior to fibrinolysis. Serial troponins should not delay reperfusion. The initial troponin may be negative early in STEMI.
reasoning: ST elevation confirms STEMI - reperfusion is time-critical
PCI superior to fibrinolysis when available within 120 min
Door-to-balloon goal <90 min for STEMI
Initial troponin often negative in first 2-3 hours

evidence.title: ACC/AHA STEMI Guidelines
evidence.cite: O'Gara PT, et al. Circulation. 2013
points: Primary PCI is preferred reperfusion strategy
Door-to-balloon time <90 minutes
Fibrinolysis if PCI not available within 120 minutes

platform.title: Practice Case: Increased Thirst and Urination

vignette:
A 68-year-old woman presents with palpitations and mild dyspnea for 2 days. She has a history of hypertension and was recently started on a new blood pressure medication 3 weeks ago.

Past Medical History: Hypertension, osteoarthritis.

Medications: Amlodipine 10 mg daily (started 3 weeks ago, previously on lisinopril), ibuprofen as needed.

Allergies: ACE inhibitors (cough).

Vital Signs
BP 142/88 mm Hg
HR 142 bpm (irregularly irregular)
RR 18/min
SpO₂ 97% on RA

Physical Examination: Alert, comfortable at rest. Irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmurs. Lungs clear. No peripheral edema. No jugular venous distension.

ECG: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, rate 138-148 bpm. No ST changes.

Laboratory: TSH 0.8 mIU/L (normal), BMP within normal limits, BNP 180 pg/mL.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management for rate control?

options.text: Diltiazem IV bolus followed by infusion

teaching.keyPoint: Diltiazem IV is appropriate first-line rate control for hemodynamically stable atrial fibrillation with RVR. This patient is stable (normal BP, no heart failure signs). Cardioversion is not indicated for stable AF of unclear duration without anticoagulation. Amiodarone is reserved for rhythm control or when other agents fail. Digoxin has slow onset and is less effective for rate control during activity. Flecainide is for rhythm control in patients without structural heart disease.
reasoning: Hemodynamically stable - rate control preferred initially
Duration >48 hours or unknown requires anticoagulation before cardioversion
Calcium channel blockers or beta-blockers are first-line for rate control
CHA₂DS₂-VASc score determines anticoagulation need

evidence.title: AHA/ACC/HRS AF Guidelines
evidence.cite: January CT, et al. Circulation. 2019
points: Rate control initial strategy for most patients
Beta-blockers or non-DHP CCBs first-line
Anticoagulation based on CHA₂DS₂-VASc score

platform.title: Practice Case: Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman

vignette:
A 58-year-old man with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF, EF 30%) presents for routine follow-up. He reports mild dyspnea with moderate exertion but can climb two flights of stairs. He sleeps on one pillow and denies paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea or lower extremity edema.

Past Medical History: HFrEF (ischemic cardiomyopathy, EF 30%), prior MI 3 years ago, hypertension, type 2 diabetes.

Medications: Lisinopril 20 mg daily, carvedilol 25 mg twice daily, furosemide 40 mg daily, atorvastatin 80 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Vital Signs
BP 118/72 mm Hg
HR 68 bpm

Physical Examination: JVP 7 cm, regular rhythm, no S3, lungs clear, no edema.

Laboratory: Cr 1.1 mg/dL, K 4.2 mEq/L, BNP 220 pg/mL (previously 450), eGFR 72 mL/min.

He is currently NYHA Class II and euvolemic on examination.
stem: Which medication should be added to optimize guideline-directed medical therapy?

teaching.keyPoint: Spironolactone (or eplerenone) is part of guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) for HFrEF that reduces mortality. The "4 pillars" of HFrEF therapy are: ACEi/ARB/ARNI, beta-blocker, MRA (spironolactone/eplerenone), and SGLT2 inhibitor. This patient is on ACEi and beta-blocker but missing MRA. His renal function and potassium allow safe addition. He is euvolemic, so increasing diuretics is unnecessary. Digoxin does not improve mortality. Ivabradine is for persistent sinus tachycardia on maximized beta-blocker.
reasoning: GDMT for HFrEF: ACEi/ARB/ARNI + BB + MRA + SGLT2i
MRAs reduce mortality in HFrEF with EF ≤35%
Check K and Cr before starting MRA
Consider ARNI replacement for ACEi if tolerating current therapy

evidence.title: AHA/ACC HF Guidelines 2022
evidence.cite: Heidenreich PA, et al. Circulation. 2022
points: GDMT quadruple therapy for HFrEF
MRA for EF ≤35%, Cr ≤2.5, K <5.0
SGLT2i for all HFrEF regardless of diabetes

platform.title: Practice Case: Worsening Shortness of Breath

vignette:
A 72-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a syncopal episode. His wife reports he was walking to the bathroom when he suddenly collapsed without warning. He regained consciousness within 30 seconds. He denies chest pain, palpitations, or prodromal symptoms. He has had two similar episodes in the past month.

Past Medical History: Hypertension, hyperlipidemia.

Medications: Lisinopril 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg daily.

Vital Signs
BP 138/82 mm Hg (lying), 136/80 mm Hg (standing)
HR 54 bpm

Physical Examination: Alert, oriented. Cardiovascular exam reveals bradycardia, regular rhythm, 3/6 late-peaking systolic murmur at right upper sternal border radiating to carotids, diminished and delayed carotid upstrokes bilaterally. Lungs clear.

ECG: Sinus bradycardia at 52 bpm, left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria, no ST changes.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next diagnostic study?

teaching.keyPoint: Transthoracic echocardiogram is essential to evaluate this patient with suspected aortic stenosis. The combination of syncope without prodrome, systolic murmur with classic characteristics (late-peaking, radiates to carotids), and diminished carotid upstrokes (parvus et tardus) strongly suggests severe aortic stenosis. Syncope in aortic stenosis indicates severe disease and warrants urgent evaluation for valve replacement. Echocardiography will confirm diagnosis and severity.
reasoning: Syncope + systolic murmur = rule out aortic stenosis
Parvus et tardus (weak, delayed pulse) suggests severe AS
Syncope in AS indicates severe disease - poor prognosis without intervention
Tilt table for vasovagal syncope, not cardiac structural disease

evidence.title: ACC/AHA Valvular Heart Disease Guidelines
evidence.cite: Otto CM, et al. Circulation. 2021
points: Symptomatic severe AS requires intervention
Echo to assess valve area, gradient, and LV function
Syncope, angina, or HF in AS indicate severe disease

platform.title: Practice Case: Rash and Fever After New Medication

vignette:
A 52-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with chest discomfort that started 6 hours ago. She describes it as a pressure sensation in her mid-chest, rated 4/10, not clearly related to exertion. She has no radiation, dyspnea, or diaphoresis.

Past Medical History: Hypertension, prediabetes (HbA1c 6.2%), obesity (BMI 32).

Medications: Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Family History: Father had MI at age 68.

Social History: Never smoker.

Vital Signs
BP 142/88 mm Hg
HR 78 bpm
SpO₂ 98% on RA

Physical Examination: No distress. Cardiovascular and lung exams normal. No chest wall tenderness.

ECG: Normal sinus rhythm, no ST changes, no T-wave abnormalities.

Initial Troponin I: <0.01 ng/mL (reference <0.04 ng/mL).
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit for serial troponins and observation

teaching.keyPoint: Repeat troponin in 3-6 hours is indicated to rule out NSTEMI. A single negative troponin does not exclude ACS, as troponin may take 3-6 hours to rise after myocardial injury. This patient has intermediate pretest probability (risk factors but atypical features). If repeat troponin is negative with non-ischemic ECG, further risk stratification (stress testing or CTA) can be considered. High-sensitivity troponin protocols may allow earlier rule-out.
reasoning: Single troponin insufficient to rule out ACS
Troponin rises 3-6 hours after injury
Serial troponins part of standard ACS evaluation
Risk stratification after ruling out ACS

evidence.title: ACC/AHA Chest Pain Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Gulati M, et al. Circulation. 2021
points: Serial troponins for intermediate-risk chest pain
High-sensitivity troponin may allow 0/1-hour or 0/3-hour protocols
Stress testing or CTA for further risk stratification

platform.title: Practice Case: Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman

vignette:
A 67-year-old man with severe COPD (FEV1 35% predicted) presents with worsening dyspnea, increased sputum production, and sputum purulence for 3 days. He uses home oxygen at 2 L/min continuously. He has had two exacerbations requiring hospitalization in the past year.

Past Medical History: COPD (GOLD Stage D), former smoker (60 pack-years, quit 2 years ago).

Medications: Tiotropium, fluticasone/vilanterol, home oxygen 2 L/min.

Vital Signs
Temp 100.4°F
BP 148/88 mm Hg
HR 102 bpm
RR 26/min
SpO₂ 88% on 2 L NC

Physical Examination: Using accessory muscles, speaking in short sentences. Diffuse expiratory wheezes and decreased breath sounds bilaterally. No peripheral edema.

ABG on 2 L NC: pH 7.32, PaCO₂ 58 mm Hg, PaO₂ 54 mm Hg, HCO₃ 30 mEq/L.

Chest X-ray: Hyperinflation, flattened diaphragms, no infiltrate.
stem: In addition to bronchodilators and antibiotics, which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase oxygen to 6 L/min via nasal cannula
Systemic corticosteroids and non-invasive positive pressure ventilation

teaching.keyPoint: Systemic corticosteroids and NIPPV are indicated for moderate-severe COPD exacerbation with hypercapnic respiratory failure. This patient has acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis (pH 7.32, elevated CO₂ with compensated bicarb). NIPPV (BiPAP) reduces work of breathing, improves gas exchange, and decreases intubation rates. Systemic steroids (prednisone 40 mg x 5 days) reduce treatment failure and hospital stay. Target SpO₂ 88-92% to avoid oxygen-induced hypercapnia.
reasoning: Acute hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH <7.35, PaCO₂ elevated)
NIPPV first-line for COPD with respiratory acidosis
Systemic steroids reduce exacerbation severity
Target SpO₂ 88-92% in COPD to avoid CO₂ retention

evidence.title: GOLD COPD Guidelines 2023
evidence.cite: Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease
points: NIPPV for respiratory acidosis in COPD exacerbation
Systemic corticosteroids 40 mg prednisone x 5 days
Antibiotics if increased sputum purulence

platform.title: Practice Case: Severe Epigastric Pain Radiating to Back

vignette:
A 58-year-old woman presents with acute dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain for 12 hours. She had knee replacement surgery 10 days ago and has been relatively immobile since.

Past Medical History: Osteoarthritis, obesity (BMI 34).

Medications: Enoxaparin 40 mg daily (prophylactic dose, discontinued 2 days ago), acetaminophen.

Vital Signs
BP 98/62 mm Hg
HR 118 bpm
RR 26/min
SpO₂ 89% on RA

Physical Examination: Anxious, tachypneic. JVP elevated. Loud P2. Clear lungs. Right calf tenderness and swelling.

ECG: Sinus tachycardia, S1Q3T3 pattern, T-wave inversions V1-V4.

Laboratory: Troponin I 0.18 ng/mL, BNP 680 pg/mL.

CT Pulmonary Angiography: Bilateral pulmonary emboli with saddle embolus at main pulmonary artery bifurcation. RV/LV ratio 1.4.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Anticoagulation with close monitoring; consider thrombolysis if deterioration

teaching.keyPoint: Anticoagulation with close monitoring is appropriate for intermediate-high risk PE. This patient has submassive PE: hemodynamically borderline (hypotensive but not in shock), RV dysfunction (RV/LV ratio >1.0, elevated troponin/BNP), but not frank hemodynamic collapse. Systemic thrombolysis has bleeding risks (especially 10 days post-surgery). The approach is therapeutic anticoagulation with ICU monitoring and rescue thrombolysis or catheter-directed therapy if clinical deterioration occurs.
reasoning: Submassive/intermediate-high risk PE: RV dysfunction without hemodynamic collapse
Systemic thrombolysis increases bleeding risk, especially post-surgery
Anticoagulation is first-line; escalate if deterioration
Catheter-directed therapy an option for intermediate-high risk

evidence.title: ESC PE Guidelines 2019
evidence.cite: Konstantinides SV, et al. Eur Heart J. 2020
points: Intermediate-high risk: RV dysfunction + positive biomarkers
Anticoagulation for all confirmed PE
Thrombolysis for high-risk (hemodynamically unstable) PE
Rescue thrombolysis if deterioration on anticoagulation

platform.title: Practice Case: Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman

vignette:
