A 78-year-old man presents from a nursing home with cough, fever, and confusion for 2 days. Staff report he has had decreased oral intake and has been more lethargic than usual.

Past Medical History: Dementia (moderate), hypertension, prior stroke with residual right-sided weakness, dysphagia requiring thickened liquids.

Medications: Amlodipine 5 mg daily, donepezil 10 mg daily.

Vital Signs
Temp 101.8°F
BP 92/58 mm Hg
HR 108 bpm
RR 28/min
SpO₂ 90% on RA

Physical Examination: Confused, lethargic. Crackles at right base. Decreased breath sounds on right.

Laboratory: WBC 14.8, BUN 38 mg/dL, Cr 1.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.0), Na 132, lactate 2.8 mmol/L.

Chest X-ray: Right lower lobe consolidation with possible aspiration component.

CURB-65 Score: Confusion (1) + Uremia BUN>19 (1) + RR≥30 (0) + BP<90 systolic (1) + Age≥65 (1) = 4
stem: What is the most appropriate disposition and antibiotic regimen?

options.text: General medical floor with ceftriaxone plus azithromycin
ICU admission with piperacillin-tazobactam plus vancomycin
ICU admission with ceftriaxone plus azithromycin plus vancomycin (if MRSA risk)

teaching.keyPoint: ICU admission with appropriate empiric antibiotics is indicated for severe CAP/healthcare-associated pneumonia. CURB-65 score of 4 indicates high mortality risk (>25%) requiring ICU consideration. This nursing home patient with aspiration risk and hypotension needs coverage for typical, atypical, and potentially resistant organisms. Ceftriaxone + azithromycin covers most CAP pathogens; add MRSA coverage (vancomycin) if risk factors present. Piperacillin-tazobactam is an alternative if aspiration/anaerobic coverage is prioritized.
reasoning: CURB-65 ≥3 suggests high mortality risk
Nursing home resident increases resistant pathogen risk
Hypotension and AKI indicate severe sepsis
Aspiration risk from dysphagia may require anaerobic coverage

evidence.title: ATS/IDSA CAP Guidelines 2019
evidence.cite: Metlay JP, et al. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2019
points: Severe CAP: β-lactam + macrolide or respiratory fluoroquinolone
ICU admission for shock or respiratory failure
Consider MRSA coverage if risk factors present

platform.title: Practice Case: Fever, Jaundice, and RUQ Pain

vignette:
A 22-year-old college student is brought to the emergency department with severe headache, fever, and neck stiffness for 12 hours. His roommate reports he has been increasingly confused over the past few hours. He was previously healthy with no significant medical history.

Past Medical History: None. Immunizations up to date including meningococcal vaccine at age 18.

Medications: None.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Vital Signs
Temp 103.2°F (39.6°C)
BP 108/68 mm Hg
HR 112 bpm
RR 20/min

Physical Examination: Toxic-appearing. Confused, oriented to name only. Neck stiffness with positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs. No papilledema. Petechial rash on trunk and lower extremities. No focal neurologic deficits.

Laboratory: WBC 18,200 with 88% neutrophils, 10% bands. Platelets 128,000.

CT head without contrast is pending and expected in 30 minutes.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Wait for CT results, then perform lumbar puncture
Empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone, vancomycin) and dexamethasone now
Lumbar puncture immediately, then antibiotics based on results
Blood cultures, then wait for CT before any treatment

teaching.keyPoint: Empiric antibiotics and dexamethasone immediately are indicated for suspected bacterial meningitis. Do not delay antibiotics for imaging or LP. This patient has classic meningitis presentation (fever, headache, neck stiffness, altered mental status) with petechial rash suggesting meningococcemia. Empiric therapy: ceftriaxone (covers pneumococcus, meningococcus) + vancomycin (MRSA, resistant pneumococcus). Dexamethasone before or with first antibiotic dose reduces mortality and neurologic sequelae, especially in pneumococcal meningitis.
reasoning: Bacterial meningitis is a medical emergency - minutes matter
Do NOT delay antibiotics for CT or LP
Petechial rash suggests Neisseria meningitidis
Dexamethasone reduces mortality if given before/with antibiotics

evidence.title: IDSA Bacterial Meningitis Guidelines 2017
evidence.cite: Tunkel AR, et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2017
points: Empiric antibiotics within 1 hour of presentation
Dexamethasone before or with first antibiotic dose
CT before LP only if focal deficits, papilledema, or immunocompromised
LP should not delay antibiotics

platform.title: Practice Case: Fatigue, Missed Periods, and Stress Fracture

vignette:
A 34-year-old woman with epilepsy is brought to the emergency department after being found having a generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home. EMS reports the seizure has been ongoing for approximately 12 minutes despite receiving lorazepam 4 mg IV en route.

Past Medical History: Epilepsy (focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizures), diagnosed 5 years ago. Last seizure was 8 months ago.

Medications: Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily.

Allergies: Phenytoin (Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

Vital Signs
BP 168/102 mm Hg
HR 124 bpm
SpO₂ 94% on 4 L NC
Temp 99.8°F

Current Status: Continues with generalized tonic-clonic movements. Not following commands. Airway maintained with positioning.

Point-of-care glucose: 118 mg/dL.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

teaching.keyPoint: Levetiracetam IV or another second-line agent is indicated for benzodiazepine-refractory status epilepticus. After adequate benzodiazepine dosing (lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg or 4 mg), a second-line antiepileptic should be given. Options include levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, or valproic acid. Fosphenytoin is contraindicated given her history of Stevens-Johnson syndrome with phenytoin (cross-reactivity). Levetiracetam has a favorable safety profile. Propofol is third-line for refractory status epilepticus after second-line agents fail.
reasoning: Status epilepticus: seizure >5 min or recurrent without recovery
Benzodiazepine-refractory after adequate initial dosing
Phenytoin allergy (SJS) contraindicates fosphenytoin
Second-line options: levetiracetam, valproate, lacosamide

evidence.title: AES Status Epilepticus Guidelines 2016
evidence.cite: Glauser T, et al. Epilepsy Curr. 2016
points: Benzodiazepine first-line
Second-line: fosphenytoin, valproate, or levetiracetam equally effective
Avoid fosphenytoin if phenytoin allergy
Third-line: anesthetic doses of propofol, midazolam, or pentobarbital

platform.title: Practice Case: Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman

vignette:
A 45-year-old woman presents with the "worst headache of her life" that started suddenly 2 hours ago while she was exercising. She describes it as a thunderclap headache reaching maximum intensity within seconds. She has had nausea and one episode of vomiting. No prior similar headaches.

Past Medical History: Migraine headaches (different character, gradual onset with aura), hypertension.

Medications: Lisinopril 10 mg daily, sumatriptan as needed for migraines.

Family History: Mother had a "brain aneurysm" at age 52.

Vital Signs
BP 162/98 mm Hg
HR 88 bpm
Temp 98.6°F

Physical Examination: Alert, in moderate distress from pain. Mild photophobia. No neck stiffness. No focal neurologic deficits. Fundoscopic exam normal.

CT Head without contrast: No hemorrhage, no mass, no hydrocephalus. Normal study.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer sumatriptan for presumed severe migraine
Reassure and discharge with neurology follow-up
MRI brain with and without contrast
CT angiography only if LP is positive

teaching.keyPoint: Lumbar puncture is required to rule out subarachnoid hemorrhage when CT is negative but clinical suspicion remains high. Thunderclap headache reaching maximum intensity within seconds is classic for SAH until proven otherwise. CT sensitivity for SAH decreases over time (>98% at 6 hours, ~90% at 24 hours). LP looking for xanthochromia (bilirubin from RBC breakdown) can detect SAH missed by CT. Family history of aneurysm increases her risk. Do NOT give sumatriptan (vasoconstrictor) until SAH excluded.
reasoning: Thunderclap headache = SAH until proven otherwise
CT sensitivity decreases over time (93% at 24h)
LP for xanthochromia if CT negative
Family history of aneurysm is a risk factor

evidence.title: AHA/ASA SAH Guidelines
evidence.cite: Connolly ES, et al. Stroke. 2012
points: CT followed by LP if negative for thunderclap headache
Xanthochromia indicates prior hemorrhage
CTA or conventional angiography to identify aneurysm
Early aneurysm treatment to prevent rebleeding

platform.title: Practice Case: Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman

vignette:
A 62-year-old man with cirrhosis secondary to hepatitis C presents with hematemesis. He reports vomiting approximately 500 mL of bright red blood over the past 2 hours. He has known esophageal varices and had variceal banding 6 months ago.

Past Medical History: Cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B), hepatitis C (treated, SVR achieved), prior variceal bleeding.

Medications: Propranolol 40 mg twice daily, lactulose, rifaximin.

Vital Signs
BP 88/54 mm Hg
HR 118 bpm
RR 22/min

Physical Examination: Pale, diaphoretic. Abdomen with ascites, no tenderness. Spider angiomata present.

Laboratory: Hgb 7.2 g/dL (baseline 10.5), INR 1.8, platelets 68,000.
stem: In addition to IV access and fluid resuscitation, which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Octreotide infusion, ceftriaxone, and blood transfusion with restrictive threshold
Fresh frozen plasma and platelet transfusion

teaching.keyPoint: Octreotide, prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone), and restrictive transfusion are critical initial steps in variceal bleeding. Octreotide reduces portal pressure. Antibiotics reduce mortality and rebleeding (ceftriaxone 1g IV daily x 7 days). Transfuse to target Hgb 7-8 g/dL (restrictive strategy reduces mortality vs liberal). Endoscopy within 12 hours after initial resuscitation. FFP/platelets do not improve outcomes in cirrhosis. TIPS is rescue therapy if endoscopy fails.
reasoning: Variceal bleeding: octreotide + antibiotics + restrictive transfusion
Antibiotics reduce mortality in cirrhotic GI bleed
Target Hgb 7-8 g/dL (overtransfusion increases portal pressure)
EGD within 12 hours after resuscitation

evidence.title: AASLD Practice Guidance: Varices and Variceal Hemorrhage
evidence.cite: Garcia-Tsao G, et al. Hepatology. 2017
points: Vasoactive drugs (octreotide/terlipressin) for up to 5 days
Prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone preferred)
Restrictive transfusion strategy
EGD within 12 hours

vignette:
A 48-year-old man presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back for 18 hours. He admits to heavy alcohol use. Pain is constant, 9/10, worse when lying flat.

Past Medical History: Alcohol use disorder, prior episode of pancreatitis 2 years ago.

Social History: Drinks 8-10 beers daily for 20 years.

Vital Signs
Temp 100.6°F
BP 98/62 mm Hg
HR 112 bpm
RR 24/min
SpO₂ 93% on RA

Physical Examination: Appears uncomfortable, lying still. Abdomen distended, diffusely tender, especially in epigastrium, decreased bowel sounds, no rebound.

Laboratory: WBC 16,800, Hct 48%, BUN 32, Cr 1.6, Glucose 186, Ca 7.8, AST 128, LDH 420, Lipase 1,850 U/L.

BISAP Score: BUN >25 (1) + Impaired mental status (0) + SIRS (1) + Age >60 (0) + Pleural effusion (pending) = 2+
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: NPO, IV fluids, pain control, and CT abdomen immediately
Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (250-500 mL/hr LR) with pain control
Empiric antibiotics for infected pancreatic necrosis

teaching.keyPoint: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation is the cornerstone of acute pancreatitis management. Goal-directed fluid therapy (250-500 mL/hr of lactated Ringer's, targeting urine output, HR, and hematocrit) reduces necrosis and organ failure. CT is not needed for diagnosis (lipase >3x ULN is diagnostic) and should be deferred 72+ hours to assess necrosis. ERCP only if biliary obstruction (cholangitis or persistent obstruction). Prophylactic antibiotics not recommended. Early enteral nutrition preferred over TPN.
reasoning: Aggressive early fluid resuscitation reduces complications
Lactated Ringer's preferred (may reduce SIRS)
CT at 72+ hours if concern for necrosis
Early enteral nutrition when tolerated

evidence.title: ACG Acute Pancreatitis Guidelines 2013
evidence.cite: Tenner S, et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013
points: Aggressive fluid resuscitation in first 12-24 hours
Lactated Ringer's preferred crystalloid
No role for prophylactic antibiotics
Early oral feeding when tolerated

platform.title: Practice Case: Severe Epigastric Pain Radiating to Back

vignette:
A 68-year-old woman presents with crampy abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and obstipation for 24 hours. She had a hysterectomy 15 years ago.

Past Medical History: Total abdominal hysterectomy, appendectomy.

Vital Signs
Temp 99.2°F
BP 118/72 mm Hg
HR 96 bpm

Physical Examination: Abdomen distended, tympanic, diffusely tender without peritoneal signs. High-pitched bowel sounds. Well-healed surgical scars.

Laboratory: WBC 11,200, BMP with mild hypokalemia (K 3.2).

CT Abdomen: Dilated small bowel loops up to 4.5 cm with decompressed distal ileum and colon. Transition point in right lower quadrant with adhesive band. No free air, no bowel wall thickening, no pneumatosis.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: NPO, nasogastric tube decompression, IV fluids, and serial abdominal exams
Oral contrast challenge and repeat imaging in 24 hours
Immediate surgical consultation for operative intervention

teaching.keyPoint: Conservative management with NGT decompression is appropriate for adhesive small bowel obstruction without signs of strangulation or complete obstruction. This patient has partial SBO from adhesions (most common cause, given surgical history) without peritonitis, fever, leukocytosis, or CT signs of ischemia. 65-80% of adhesive SBO resolves with conservative management. Surgery if no improvement in 48-72 hours, or immediately if signs of strangulation (fever, peritonitis, tachycardia, acidosis, CT showing bowel wall changes).
reasoning: Adhesive SBO without strangulation can be managed conservatively
Signs of strangulation require emergent surgery
NGT decompression, IV fluids, electrolyte correction
Surgery if no resolution in 48-72 hours

evidence.title: EAST SBO Guidelines
evidence.cite: Maung AA, et al. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2012
points: Initial nonoperative management for partial adhesive SBO
Operative intervention for complete obstruction or strangulation signs
Water-soluble contrast may be therapeutic and prognostic
Serial exams essential to detect deterioration

platform.title: Practice Case: Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman

vignette:
A 35-year-old woman presents with dysuria, frequency, urgency, and suprapubic discomfort for 2 days. She has had 4 UTIs in the past year, the most recent 6 weeks ago treated with ciprofloxacin.

Past Medical History: Recurrent UTIs, no other medical conditions.

Medications: None currently. Recent ciprofloxacin course completed 6 weeks ago.

Allergies: Penicillin (hives as a child).

Social History: Sexually active, new partner in last 3 months. Uses diaphragm for contraception.

Vital Signs
Temp 98.8°F
BP 118/74 mm Hg
HR 78 bpm

Physical Examination: Suprapubic tenderness. No CVA tenderness. No vaginal discharge.

Urinalysis: Large leukocyte esterase, positive nitrites, >50 WBC/hpf, many bacteria.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate empiric treatment?

options.text: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 7 days
Nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for 5 days
Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg twice daily for 7 days
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS twice daily for 3 days

teaching.keyPoint: Nitrofurantoin is an appropriate first-line agent for uncomplicated cystitis. Fluoroquinolones should be reserved for more serious infections due to resistance concerns and FDA warnings. Recent ciprofloxacin use increases resistance risk. TMP-SMX resistance is >20% in many areas. Penicillin allergy described (hives as a child) is low-risk and cross-reactivity with cephalosporins is <2%, but amoxicillin-clavulanate is not first-line for uncomplicated UTI anyway. Fosfomycin is an option but may have lower efficacy than nitrofurantoin.
reasoning: Nitrofurantoin or TMP-SMX first-line for uncomplicated cystitis
Avoid fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated UTI when alternatives exist
Recent fluoroquinolone use increases resistance risk
Consider local resistance patterns

evidence.title: IDSA Uncomplicated UTI Guidelines 2010
evidence.cite: Gupta K, et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2011
points: Nitrofurantoin, TMP-SMX, or fosfomycin first-line
Fluoroquinolones reserved for when other options inappropriate
Consider local resistance >20% when choosing TMP-SMX
5-day nitrofurantoin equivalent to 3-day TMP-SMX

platform.title: Practice Case: Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman

vignette:
A 52-year-old man with diabetes presents with a painful, red, swollen left lower leg for 3 days. He scraped his shin on a boat dock 5 days ago. The redness has been spreading despite starting cephalexin 500 mg four times daily 2 days ago.

Past Medical History: Type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.8%), obesity, venous stasis.

Medications: Metformin 1000 mg twice daily, cephalexin started 2 days ago.

Vital Signs
Temp 101.2°F
BP 138/86 mm Hg
HR 96 bpm

Physical Examination: Left lower leg with 15 cm × 12 cm area of erythema, warmth, and tenderness extending from mid-shin to ankle. Demarcated borders marked. No crepitus. No fluctuance or purulent drainage. Mild edema. Interdigital tinea pedis present.

Laboratory: WBC 14,200, Cr 1.1, glucose 218.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate change in management?

options.text: Obtain MRI to rule out osteomyelitis

teaching.keyPoint: IV vancomycin is appropriate for this patient with cellulitis failing oral antibiotics. Risk factors for treatment failure and MRSA include diabetes, prior antibiotics, and spreading infection on therapy. Hospitalization is indicated for systemic signs (fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis) and failure of oral therapy. Vancomycin provides MRSA coverage. No abscess or fluctuance present, so I&D not indicated. MRI would be considered if concern for deeper infection (osteomyelitis, necrotizing fasciitis), but clinical picture is consistent with cellulitis.
reasoning: Cellulitis progressing despite oral cephalexin
Diabetes and prior antibiotics increase MRSA risk
Systemic signs (fever, leukocytosis) indicate need for IV antibiotics
No fluctuance = no abscess requiring drainage

evidence.title: IDSA Skin and Soft Tissue Infections Guidelines 2014
evidence.cite: Stevens DL, et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2014
points: Purulent cellulitis: cover MRSA (TMP-SMX, doxycycline)
Nonpurulent cellulitis: beta-lactam usually adequate
IV therapy for treatment failure, systemic toxicity, or immunocompromise
Consider MRSA if risk factors or failure of beta-lactam

platform.title: Practice Case: Increased Thirst and Urination

vignette:
A 42-year-old man with a history of IV drug use presents with fever, fatigue, and myalgias for 2 weeks. He also reports worsening dyspnea and a painful nodule on his finger.

Past Medical History: Hepatitis C, IV heroin use (ongoing).

Social History: Active IV heroin use. Homeless, staying in shelter.

Vital Signs
Temp 102.4°F
BP 108/62 mm Hg
HR 108 bpm
SpO₂ 94% on RA

Physical Examination: Ill-appearing. Track marks on arms. Holosystolic murmur best heard at left lower sternal border, increasing with inspiration. Tender erythematous nodule on right index finger pulp. Scattered petechiae on conjunctivae.

Laboratory: WBC 16,800, Hgb 10.2, ESR 85, CRP 142.

Chest X-ray: Multiple bilateral nodular opacities with cavitation.

Echocardiogram: 1.8 cm vegetation on tricuspid valve with moderate tricuspid regurgitation.

Blood cultures: 4/4 bottles growing gram-positive cocci in clusters at 12 hours.
stem: Based on the modified Duke criteria, this patient has which of the following?

options.text: Cannot be determined until culture speciation

teaching.keyPoint: Definite infective endocarditis by modified Duke criteria requires 2 major, or 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor criteria. This patient has: MAJOR: (1) Positive blood cultures with typical organism (staph), (2) Echo with vegetation. MINOR: (1) Predisposing condition (IVDU), (2) Fever, (3) Vascular phenomena (septic pulmonary emboli), (4) Immunologic phenomena (Osler node on finger). With 2 major + 4 minor criteria, this is definite endocarditis.
reasoning: Duke criteria: 2 major OR 1 major + 3 minor OR 5 minor
Major: positive blood cultures, echo findings
Minor: predisposition, fever, vascular phenomena, immunologic phenomena, microbiologic evidence
Right-sided endocarditis common in IVDU (tricuspid valve)

evidence.title: Modified Duke Criteria for IE
evidence.cite: Li JS, et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2000
points: Definite IE: 2 major or 1 major + 3 minor or 5 minor
Major criteria: typical organisms, echo findings
Minor: predisposition, fever >38°C, vascular/immunologic phenomena
Staph aureus most common in IVDU endocarditis

platform.title: Practice Case: Fever, Jaundice, and RUQ Pain

vignette:
A 72-year-old man is admitted for pneumonia. On hospital day 3, his creatinine has risen from 1.1 mg/dL to 2.4 mg/dL. He received IV contrast for CT chest on day 1. Urine output has been 30-40 mL/hr.

Past Medical History: Hypertension, type 2 diabetes, CKD stage 3a (baseline Cr 1.4).

Medications: Lisinopril 20 mg daily (held on admission), metformin (held), ceftriaxone, azithromycin, NS at 75 mL/hr.

Vital Signs
BP 118/72 mm Hg
HR 82 bpm

Physical Examination: No JVD. Lungs with crackles at right base. Trace pedal edema. Mucous membranes moist.

Laboratory: Cr 2.4 (from 1.1 on admission, baseline 1.4), BUN 42, K 5.2.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?

options.text: Urinalysis with microscopy and urine electrolytes

teaching.keyPoint: Urinalysis with microscopy and urine electrolytes is the first step in evaluating AKI to distinguish prerenal, intrinsic renal, and postrenal causes. FeNa <1% with bland sediment suggests prerenal or contrast nephropathy. Muddy brown casts indicate ATN. RBC casts suggest glomerulonephritis. This patient has multiple potential causes: contrast (ATN), dehydration (prerenal), or ceftriaxone-induced interstitial nephritis. Urine studies guide further workup. Renal ultrasound for obstruction if post-renal suspected, but less likely with good urine output.
reasoning: AKI evaluation: prerenal vs intrinsic vs postrenal
FeNa <1% suggests prerenal; >2% suggests ATN
Urine microscopy: muddy brown casts (ATN), RBC casts (GN), WBC casts (AIN)
Multiple potential causes in this patient

evidence.title: KDIGO AKI Guidelines
evidence.cite: Kidney Int Suppl. 2012
points: Urinalysis and urine electrolytes in AKI evaluation
FeNa interpretation limited by diuretics
Renal ultrasound if obstruction suspected
Consider nephrology referral if etiology unclear

vignette:
A 68-year-old man with CKD stage 4 and heart failure presents for routine follow-up. He has been compliant with his low-potassium diet. He feels well with no complaints.

Past Medical History: CKD stage 4 (Cr 3.2), HFrEF (EF 35%), diabetes, hypertension.

Medications: Sacubitril-valsartan, carvedilol, spironolactone 25 mg daily, furosemide, empagliflozin, insulin.

Vital Signs
BP 122/74 mm Hg
HR 68 bpm

Laboratory: K 6.2 mEq/L (was 5.4 one month ago), Cr 3.4, BUN 58.

ECG: Normal sinus rhythm, no peaked T waves, QRS 98 ms.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

teaching.keyPoint: Discontinue spironolactone is appropriate for asymptomatic hyperkalemia without ECG changes in a patient on multiple drugs that raise potassium. This patient is on triple therapy that increases potassium: ARNI (sacubitril-valsartan), MRA (spironolactone), and SGLT2i (though this actually may help). With progressive CKD and K >6.0, the MRA should be held/discontinued. No emergent treatment needed without ECG changes or symptoms. Calcium gluconate is for cardiac membrane stabilization when ECG changes present. Consider potassium binders (patiromer, sodium zirconium cyclosilicate) if want to continue GDMT.
reasoning: Hyperkalemia without ECG changes = not emergent
Multiple medications contributing: ARNI + MRA
Progressive CKD limits potassium excretion
Consider newer K binders to maintain GDMT if possible

evidence.title: KDIGO CKD Management
evidence.cite: Kidney Int Suppl. 2013
points: Monitor K with RAAS inhibitors in CKD
Discontinue or reduce dose if K >5.5 with rising trend
Newer potassium binders may allow continuation of GDMT
ECG changes warrant emergent treatment

platform.title: Practice Case: Worsening Shortness of Breath

vignette:
A 28-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain for 1 day. She ran out of insulin 2 days ago. She has been drinking fluids but has been unable to keep anything down.

Past Medical History: Type 1 diabetes diagnosed at age 12, no prior DKA.

Medications: Insulin glargine and insulin lispro (ran out).

Vital Signs
Temp 98.6°F
BP 98/62 mm Hg
HR 112 bpm
RR 28/min (Kussmaul)

Physical Examination: Dry mucous membranes, fruity breath, diffuse abdominal tenderness without guarding. Alert but lethargic.

Laboratory: Glucose 486 mg/dL, Na 128 mEq/L, K 5.8 mEq/L, Cl 92 mEq/L, CO₂ 10 mEq/L, BUN 32, Cr 1.4, pH 7.18, pCO₂ 22, calculated anion gap 26, serum ketones large.
stem: After initial IV fluid bolus, which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Regular insulin 0.1 units/kg IV bolus then 0.1 units/kg/hr infusion
Hold insulin until potassium is below 5.0
Add potassium to IV fluids immediately

teaching.keyPoint: Regular insulin IV infusion is appropriate after initial fluid resuscitation. Start insulin after confirming potassium is >3.3 mEq/L (it is 5.8 here). While initial K is elevated, it will drop rapidly with insulin therapy, so DO NOT add K to fluids yet, but monitor closely (q1-2h). Sodium bicarbonate is only indicated if pH <6.9 or severe hyperkalemia with ECG changes. Subcutaneous insulin is for mild DKA or after resolution; IV insulin allows precise titration.
reasoning: DKA treatment: fluids → insulin → potassium replacement → monitor
Start insulin when K >3.3 (even if elevated, K will drop)
Do not add K initially if >5.2, but monitor frequently
Bicarbonate only if pH <6.9 or life-threatening hyperkalemia

evidence.title: ADA DKA Management
evidence.cite: Diabetes Care. 2009
points: Initial fluid: NS 15-20 mL/kg/hr or 1-1.5 L first hour
Insulin 0.1 U/kg/hr after K >3.3
Add K when <5.2 to maintain 4-5 mEq/L
Bicarbonate only if pH <6.9

platform.title: Practice Case: Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman

vignette:
A 38-year-old woman with Graves' disease presents with fever, agitation, and palpitations. She stopped taking her methimazole 3 weeks ago due to side effects. She had a dental extraction 2 days ago.

Past Medical History: Graves' disease diagnosed 1 year ago.

Medications: Methimazole (discontinued 3 weeks ago).

Vital Signs
Temp 104.2°F (40.1°C)
BP 168/88 mm Hg
HR 148 bpm (irregularly irregular)
RR 24/min

Physical Examination: Agitated, tremulous, diaphoretic. Lid lag and exophthalmos. Diffuse goiter with bruit. Irregularly irregular rhythm. Hyperreflexia.

Laboratory: TSH <0.01 mIU/L, Free T4 5.8 ng/dL (normal 0.8-1.8).

Burch-Wartofsky Score: 55 (highly suggestive of thyroid storm).
stem: Which of the following is the correct order of medication administration?

options.text: Propylthiouracil → propranolol → saturated potassium iodide → hydrocortisone
Propranolol → propylthiouracil → hydrocortisone → saturated potassium iodide (1 hour after PTU)

teaching.keyPoint: Propranolol → PTU → hydrocortisone → iodine (delayed) is the correct sequence. Beta-blockers first for symptom control and to block T4→T3 conversion. PTU (preferred over methimazole in storm because it also blocks conversion) to stop new hormone synthesis. Hydrocortisone for possible adrenal insufficiency and to block conversion. SSKI/Lugol's iodine MUST be given 1 hour AFTER thionamide to prevent iodine from being used to make more thyroid hormone (Jod-Basedow effect). Radioactive iodine is contraindicated acutely.
reasoning: Thyroid storm is life-threatening - mortality 10-30%
Beta-blocker first for symptom control
PTU preferred: blocks synthesis AND peripheral conversion
Iodine must wait until synthesis blocked (1 hour after PTU)

evidence.title: ATA Hyperthyroidism Guidelines
evidence.cite: Ross DS, et al. Thyroid. 2016
points: Beta-blocker for symptoms
PTU preferred in thyroid storm
Glucocorticoids reduce T4→T3 conversion
Iodine after thionamide to prevent hormone synthesis from iodine

platform.title: Practice Case: Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman

vignette:
A 42-year-old woman presents with depressed mood, anhedonia, poor concentration, fatigue, and insomnia for 8 weeks. She has lost 10 pounds without trying. She denies suicidal ideation. This is her first episode.

Past Medical History: Hypertension.

Medications: Amlodipine 5 mg daily.

PHQ-9 Score: 18 (moderately severe depression).

Mental Status Exam: Psychomotor slowing, flat affect, no psychotic features, no SI/HI.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?

teaching.keyPoint: SSRI monotherapy is first-line for moderate-severe depression. SSRIs have favorable side effect profile and safety in overdose. For moderate-severe depression (PHQ-9 15-19), pharmacotherapy is recommended; combination with therapy is ideal but medication alone is appropriate. TCAs have more side effects and cardiac risks. ECT reserved for treatment-resistant or severe cases with psychosis/catatonia. CBT alone may be insufficient for moderately severe depression.
reasoning: SSRIs first-line for MDD: efficacy, safety, tolerability
Moderately severe depression warrants pharmacotherapy
TCAs reserved for refractory cases due to side effects
ECT for treatment-resistant or severe/psychotic depression

evidence.title: APA MDD Guidelines
evidence.cite: Gelenberg AJ, et al. Am J Psychiatry. 2010
points: SSRIs, SNRIs, or bupropion first-line
Combined medication + psychotherapy optimal
Monitor for suicidality especially in young adults
Adequate trial: 4-8 weeks at therapeutic dose

platform.title: Practice Case: Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman

vignette:
A 28-year-old man is brought to the ED by police after being found directing traffic in the middle of the night wearing minimal clothing. He is talking rapidly, is easily distracted, and reports he only needs 2 hours of sleep. He believes he has special powers. His family reports this started 5 days ago after he stopped taking his medications. He has a history of bipolar I disorder.

Past Medical History: Bipolar I disorder with two prior manic episodes.

Medications: Lithium 900 mg daily (stopped 2 weeks ago).

Mental Status Exam: Pressured speech, flight of ideas, grandiose delusions, irritable when redirected, poor insight.

Laboratory: Lithium level undetectable. Urine drug screen negative. TSH, BMP normal.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Haloperidol IM for acute agitation, then restart mood stabilizer
Lithium plus an atypical antipsychotic (e.g., olanzapine or quetiapine)

teaching.keyPoint: Combination of mood stabilizer plus atypical antipsychotic is most effective for acute mania. Lithium alone has delayed onset (7-14 days) and patient is acutely manic with psychotic features. Adding an atypical (olanzapine, quetiapine, risperidone) provides faster symptom control. SSRIs are contraindicated in acute mania as they can worsen symptoms. Haloperidol may be needed for acute agitation but is not a mood stabilizer for ongoing treatment.
reasoning: Acute mania: combination therapy more effective than monotherapy
Lithium onset delayed 1-2 weeks
Antipsychotics provide faster control of psychosis and agitation
SSRIs contraindicated in acute mania - can trigger/worsen episode

evidence.title: APA Bipolar Guidelines
evidence.cite: Yatham LN, et al. Bipolar Disord. 2018
points: Combination therapy for acute mania
Lithium + atypical antipsychotic or valproate + antipsychotic
Avoid antidepressants in acute mania
Continue mood stabilizer for maintenance

platform.title: Practice Case: Rash and Fever After New Medication

vignette:
A 55-year-old man admitted for pneumonia becomes increasingly agitated and tremulous on hospital day 2. He is diaphoretic and reports seeing bugs crawling on the walls. He was drinking a pint of vodka daily until admission. His last drink was 36 hours ago.

Past Medical History: Alcohol use disorder, hypertension.

Vital Signs
Temp 100.8°F
BP 168/102 mm Hg
HR 118 bpm

Physical Examination: Diaphoretic, tremulous, agitated, oriented to person only. Visual hallucinations present.

CIWA-Ar Score: 28 (severe withdrawal).
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment?

options.text: Lorazepam IV with symptom-triggered dosing protocol

teaching.keyPoint: Benzodiazepines (lorazepam IV) are first-line for alcohol withdrawal and delirium tremens. Symptom-triggered dosing based on CIWA-Ar score reduces total benzodiazepine dose and duration. Lorazepam preferred in liver disease (shorter half-life, no active metabolites). Haloperidol lowers seizure threshold and should be avoided as monotherapy. Propranolol masks symptoms but doesnt prevent seizures or DTs. This patient has DT (hallucinations, autonomic instability, altered sensorium) which has 5-15% mortality without treatment.
reasoning: Benzodiazepines prevent seizures and DT
CIWA-Ar guided dosing is evidence-based
Lorazepam preferred in liver disease
Antipsychotics lower seizure threshold - avoid alone

evidence.title: ASAM Alcohol Withdrawal Guidelines
evidence.cite: Mayo-Smith MF. JAMA. 1997
points: Benzodiazepines first-line
Symptom-triggered superior to fixed dosing
Thiamine before glucose
DT mortality 5-15% untreated, <1% with treatment

vignette:
A 28-year-old woman (G2P1) presents with vaginal spotting and left lower quadrant pain for 3 days. Her last menstrual period was 7 weeks ago. She has a history of chlamydia infection treated 3 years ago.

Past Medical History: Previous normal vaginal delivery, treated chlamydia.

Medications: Prenatal vitamins.

Vital Signs
BP 118/72 mm Hg
HR 82 bpm

Physical Examination: Mild left adnexal tenderness, no rebound, no cervical motion tenderness. Closed cervical os.

Laboratory: β-hCG 2,400 mIU/mL, Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL, Rh positive.

Transvaginal Ultrasound: Empty uterus, 2.2 cm left adnexal mass with no fetal cardiac activity, small amount of free fluid in pelvis.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Expectant management with serial β-hCG levels

teaching.keyPoint: Methotrexate is appropriate for hemodynamically stable ectopic pregnancy meeting criteria: β-hCG <5000, no fetal cardiac activity, mass <3.5 cm, patient able to follow-up. This patient meets criteria. Expectant management only if β-hCG declining or very low. Surgery for ruptured ectopic, hemodynamic instability, or contraindications to methotrexate. Must confirm no intrauterine pregnancy (discriminatory zone ~1500-2000 for TVUS).
reasoning: Ectopic pregnancy: stable patient may receive methotrexate
Criteria: β-hCG <5000, no FHR, mass <3.5-4cm, compliant patient
Surgery if ruptured, unstable, or methotrexate contraindicated
D&C not indicated - pregnancy is ectopic, not intrauterine

evidence.title: ACOG Ectopic Pregnancy Guidelines
evidence.cite: ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 193
points: Methotrexate for stable ectopic meeting criteria
Single-dose regimen: 50 mg/m²
Monitor β-hCG on days 4 and 7
Surgery if β-hCG does not decline appropriately

platform.title: Practice Case: Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman

vignette:
A 32-year-old woman (G1P0) at 34 weeks gestation presents with headache and visual changes. She has a history of chronic hypertension controlled on labetalol. Her prenatal care has been uncomplicated until now.

Past Medical History: Chronic hypertension.

Medications: Labetalol 200 mg twice daily, prenatal vitamins.

Vital Signs
BP 168/108 mm Hg (repeated: 172/110)
HR 88 bpm

Physical Examination: Alert, complaining of headache. 3+ pitting edema bilateral lower extremities. Fundal height appropriate, fetal heart tones 140s.

Laboratory: Cr 1.1 mg/dL, platelets 98,000, AST 142, ALT 128, uric acid 8.2.

Urinalysis: 3+ protein.
stem: In addition to blood pressure control, which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Outpatient monitoring with twice-weekly BP checks
Magnesium sulfate and betamethasone, then delivery at 37 weeks
Magnesium sulfate and plan for delivery after corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity
Increase labetalol and continue pregnancy to term

teaching.keyPoint: Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis and corticosteroids, then delivery is appropriate for preeclampsia with severe features at 34 weeks. Severe features include: BP ≥160/110, thrombocytopenia, elevated transaminases, symptoms (headache, visual changes). Magnesium prevents eclampsia. Betamethasone for fetal lung maturity at <34 weeks (some give at 34-36w6d). Delivery is definitive treatment - aim for delivery within 24-48 hours after steroids if possible, but do not delay if maternal/fetal status deteriorates.
reasoning: Severe preeclampsia: BP ≥160/110, symptoms, labs
Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis
Corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity <37 weeks
Delivery is definitive treatment - timing based on severity and gestational age

evidence.title: ACOG Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy
evidence.cite: ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 222
points: Severe features warrant delivery consideration at ≥34 weeks
Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis
Corticosteroids if <34 weeks (consider up to 36+6)
Do not delay delivery for steroids if unstable

platform.title: Practice Case: Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman

vignette:
A 4-month-old previously healthy infant is brought to the ED with cough, nasal congestion, and increased work of breathing for 2 days. Her older sibling had a cold last week. She is breast-fed and has been feeding poorly for the past day.

Past Medical History: Full-term, vaccinations up to date.

Vital Signs
Temp 100.4°F
HR 158 bpm
RR 58/min
SpO₂ 92% on RA

Physical Examination: Alert, moderate intercostal and subcostal retractions, nasal flaring. Diffuse wheezes and crackles bilaterally. Wet-sounding cough.

Respiratory Panel: RSV positive.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Admit for supportive care with supplemental oxygen and nasal suctioning

teaching.keyPoint: Supportive care is the mainstay of bronchiolitis treatment. This infant needs admission for hypoxemia (SpO₂ 92%), moderate respiratory distress, and poor feeding. Treatment includes supplemental O₂ to maintain SpO₂ >90%, nasal suctioning, and ensuring adequate hydration (IV if not feeding). Bronchodilators and corticosteroids are NOT recommended - studies show no benefit. Ribavirin is not routinely used. Antibiotics only if bacterial superinfection suspected.
reasoning: Bronchiolitis: supportive care is evidence-based treatment
Bronchodilators and steroids NOT recommended (no benefit)
Admit for: hypoxemia, moderate-severe distress, poor feeding, young age
Palivizumab for prevention in high-risk infants, not treatment

evidence.title: AAP Bronchiolitis Guidelines 2014
evidence.cite: Ralston SL, et al. Pediatrics. 2014
points: Supportive care: O₂, hydration, suctioning
Do not use bronchodilators or corticosteroids routinely
Do not obtain CXR or use antibiotics routinely
Admit if SpO₂ <90%, respiratory distress, dehydration

platform.title: Practice Case: Cough and Fast Breathing in an Infant

vignette:
A 28-day-old full-term male infant is brought to the ED with fever noted at home (rectal 100.8°F). He has been feeding well and appears non-toxic. He was delivered vaginally without complications. Mother was GBS negative.

Birth History: Full-term, uncomplicated vaginal delivery, GBS negative, discharged day 2 of life.

Vital Signs
Temp 100.6°F (38.1°C) rectal
HR 148 bpm
RR 42/min

Physical Examination: Alert, well-appearing, normal activity. No rash, no respiratory distress, soft fontanelle, benign abdominal exam. HEENT normal.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge with close outpatient follow-up in 24 hours
Urinalysis only; if negative, discharge home
Full sepsis workup (CBC, blood culture, urinalysis/culture, LP) and empiric antibiotics
CBC and urinalysis; LP only if abnormal

teaching.keyPoint: Full sepsis workup and empiric antibiotics are required for any febrile infant ≤28 days old regardless of appearance. This age group has immature immune systems and can have serious bacterial infection (meningitis, bacteremia, UTI) despite appearing well. Workup includes CBC, blood culture, urinalysis, urine culture, and LP with CSF studies. Empiric antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin or cefotaxime) until cultures negative at 36-48 hours. Clinical appearance is unreliable in neonates.
reasoning: Febrile neonate (≤28 days): high risk for serious bacterial infection
Clinical appearance unreliable in this age group
Full sepsis workup mandatory: blood, urine, CSF
Empiric antibiotics: ampicillin + gentamicin (or cefotaxime)

evidence.title: AAP Febrile Infant Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Pantell RH, et al. Pediatrics. 2021
points: 0-21 days: full workup including LP, empiric antibiotics
22-28 days: consider inflammatory markers to risk stratify
Urinalysis via catheter or suprapubic aspiration
HSV testing if risk factors or concerning findings

platform.title: Practice Case: Cough and Fast Breathing in an Infant

vignette:
A 58-year-old woman presents with left leg swelling and pain for 3 days. She had knee replacement surgery 3 weeks ago. She has no history of bleeding problems or prior VTE.

Past Medical History: Osteoarthritis, hypertension.

Medications: Lisinopril 10 mg daily. Completed rivaroxaban prophylaxis 1 week ago.

Vital Signs
BP 132/82 mm Hg
HR 78 bpm

Physical Examination: Left leg with calf swelling (3 cm larger than right), warmth, and tenderness. No erythema or skin changes.

Lower Extremity Doppler Ultrasound: Acute occlusive thrombus in left femoral and popliteal veins.

Laboratory: Cr 0.9, platelets 245,000, INR 1.0.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?

options.text: Warfarin 5 mg daily with INR goal 2-3
Rivaroxaban 15 mg twice daily for 21 days, then 20 mg daily

teaching.keyPoint: Direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) such as rivaroxaban is first-line for provoked proximal DVT. DOACs are preferred over warfarin for most patients (no bridging, no INR monitoring, comparable efficacy, better safety). Duration for provoked DVT (post-surgical) is typically 3 months. IVC filter only if anticoagulation contraindicated. Thrombolysis reserved for massive iliofemoral DVT with limb threat.
reasoning: DOACs preferred over warfarin for VTE treatment
No bridging required with DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban)
Provoked DVT: 3 months treatment
IVC filter only if anticoagulation contraindicated

evidence.title: ASH VTE Guidelines 2020
evidence.cite: Ortel TL, et al. Blood Adv. 2020
points: DOACs preferred over VKA for most patients
Rivaroxaban: 15 mg BID x 21d, then 20 mg daily
Provoked VTE: 3 months
Extended anticoagulation for unprovoked VTE

platform.title: Practice Case: Lower Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman

vignette:
A 68-year-old man presents with fatigue and dyspnea on exertion for 2 months. He denies melena, hematochezia, or hematuria. He has a history of well-controlled hypertension.

Past Medical History: Hypertension.

Medications: Lisinopril 20 mg daily.

Social History: No alcohol, no tobacco.

Physical Examination: Pale conjunctivae. No lymphadenopathy. Abdomen soft, non-tender. No hepatosplenomegaly. Rectal exam with brown stool, guaiac positive.

Laboratory: Hgb 8.2 g/dL, MCV 72 fL, RDW 18%, WBC 6.8, platelets 380,000.

Iron Studies: Ferritin 8 ng/mL (low), Iron 25 µg/dL (low), TIBC 450 µg/dL (high), Transferrin sat 6% (low).
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Oral iron supplementation and recheck in 3 months

teaching.keyPoint: Colonoscopy is essential in this patient with iron deficiency anemia and guaiac-positive stool. In adults (especially >50 years), iron deficiency anemia is GI blood loss until proven otherwise. Must rule out colorectal cancer and other GI pathology. EGD may also be needed if colonoscopy negative. Do not simply treat with iron without investigating the source. Iron studies confirm iron deficiency (low ferritin, low iron, high TIBC, low sat).
reasoning: Iron deficiency anemia in adults = GI blood loss until proven otherwise
Guaiac positive stool mandates colonoscopy
Rule out colorectal malignancy in patients >50
Do not delay workup by empirically treating with iron

evidence.title: AGA Iron Deficiency Anemia Guidelines
evidence.cite: Ko CW, et al. Gastroenterology. 2020
points: Bidirectional endoscopy for iron deficiency anemia
Colonoscopy to rule out colorectal cancer
EGD if colonoscopy negative or symptoms suggest upper GI source
Treat underlying cause, not just iron replacement

platform.title: Practice Case: Fatigue, Missed Periods, and Stress Fracture

vignette:
A 58-year-old man presents with severe pain, swelling, and redness of his right great toe that began overnight. He rates the pain 9/10 and cannot bear weight. He had a similar episode 2 years ago that resolved with indomethacin. He has CKD stage 3.

Past Medical History: CKD stage 3 (Cr 1.8), hypertension, hyperlipidemia.

Medications: Lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily.

Social History: Drinks 2-3 beers on weekends.

Vital Signs
Temp 99.2°F
BP 148/92 mm Hg
HR 88 bpm

Physical Examination: Right first MTP joint erythematous, swollen, exquisitely tender. Unable to tolerate light touch.

Laboratory: Cr 1.9 mg/dL, WBC 11,200, uric acid 9.8 mg/dL.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment for acute gout in this patient?

options.text: Colchicine 1.2 mg then 0.6 mg one hour later

teaching.keyPoint: Prednisone is appropriate for acute gout in a patient with CKD. NSAIDs are relatively contraindicated in CKD (further renal impairment). Colchicine requires dose adjustment and can still cause toxicity in renal impairment. Systemic corticosteroids are effective and safe for acute gout. Do NOT start allopurinol during acute flare - it can worsen and prolong the attack; start after flare resolves. Intra-articular injection is an option for monoarticular disease if systemic steroids contraindicated.
reasoning: Acute gout treatment: NSAIDs, colchicine, or steroids
CKD: avoid NSAIDs, adjust colchicine dose
Steroids safe and effective alternative
Do NOT start urate-lowering therapy during acute flare

evidence.title: ACR Gout Guidelines 2020
evidence.cite: FitzGerald JD, et al. Arthritis Care Res. 2020
points: NSAIDs, colchicine, or corticosteroids for acute gout
Choice based on comorbidities and contraindications
Do not initiate ULT during acute flare
Consider prophylaxis when starting ULT

platform.title: Practice Case: Severe Epigastric Pain Radiating to Back

vignette:
A 65-year-old man with diabetes presents with fever, confusion, and hypotension. He was found to have a large sacral decubitus ulcer with surrounding cellulitis. Despite 3 liters of crystalloid, his blood pressure remains 78/48 mm Hg.

Past Medical History: Type 2 diabetes, paraplegia from spinal cord injury, chronic indwelling urinary catheter.

Vital Signs (after 3L fluid)
Temp 102.8°F
HR 124 bpm
BP 78/48 mm Hg
MAP 58 mm Hg

Laboratory: WBC 22,000, Lactate 5.8 mmol/L, Cr 2.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.2).

Broad-spectrum antibiotics have been administered. Central venous access is being obtained.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue crystalloid resuscitation until MAP >65 mm Hg

teaching.keyPoint: Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor for septic shock. This patient has septic shock (infection + hypotension refractory to fluid resuscitation + elevated lactate indicating tissue hypoperfusion). After initial fluid resuscitation (30 mL/kg), vasopressors should be initiated to maintain MAP ≥65 mm Hg. Norepinephrine is preferred over other vasopressors due to its favorable effects on splanchnic perfusion. Hydrocortisone is reserved for shock refractory to fluids AND vasopressors. Dobutamine is added if cardiac output is inadequate despite adequate filling pressures.
reasoning: Septic shock definition: sepsis + vasopressor requirement + lactate >2 despite fluids
Norepinephrine is first-line vasopressor (Surviving Sepsis Campaign)
Target MAP ≥65 mm Hg
Steroids only for refractory shock

evidence.title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021
evidence.cite: Evans L, et al. Crit Care Med. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143
points: Norepinephrine as first-line vasopressor (strong recommendation)
Target MAP 65 mm Hg initially
Add vasopressin if norepinephrine dose is escalating
Hydrocortisone for shock refractory to fluids and vasopressors

distractors.Continue crystalloid resuscitation until: This patient has already received 3L (~30 mL/kg). Excessive fluids without vasopressors can cause harm. Guidelines recommend initiating vasopressors rather than continuing large volume resuscitation.
distractors.Administer hydrocortisone 100 mg IV: Hydrocortisone is indicated for septic shock refractory to fluids AND vasopressors, not before vasopressor trial.
distractors.Start dobutamine infusion: Dobutamine is an inotrope for cardiac dysfunction, not a first-line vasopressor for septic shock. It may be added if evidence of cardiac dysfunction despite adequate perfusion pressures.
distractors.Transfuse packed red blood cells: There is no indication for transfusion without documented anemia. Hemoglobin target in sepsis is generally >7 g/dL.

vignette:
A 72-year-old woman presents to the ED with acute onset right-sided weakness and aphasia. Symptoms began 2 hours ago while eating breakfast. Her husband witnessed the onset.

Past Medical History: Hypertension, atrial fibrillation (on apixaban, last dose 36 hours ago due to GI upset), prior ischemic stroke 5 years ago with full recovery.

Vital Signs
BP 178/96 mm Hg
HR 88 bpm (irregularly irregular)

Neurological Exam: Alert, global aphasia, right facial droop, right hemiplegia (0/5 arm, 2/5 leg), right hemineglect. NIHSS: 18.

CT Head: No hemorrhage. Early loss of gray-white differentiation in left MCA territory. ASPECTS 7.

Labs: Glucose 142, Cr 1.0, INR 1.1, Platelets 198,000.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Obtain CT angiography and proceed to mechanical thrombectomy evaluation
Check anti-Xa level before any intervention
Administer idarucizumab followed by IV alteplase
Start aspirin and admit to stroke unit

teaching.keyPoint: CT angiography and thrombectomy evaluation is indicated. This patient has a severe stroke (NIHSS 18) suggesting large vessel occlusion (LVO). While she is within the tPA window, her recent apixaban use (36 hours ago, within 2-3 half-lives) creates uncertainty about anticoagulation status. Importantly, mechanical thrombectomy does NOT require normal coagulation and can proceed even in anticoagulated patients. CTA will confirm LVO and guide intervention. Anti-Xa level can help determine tPA eligibility but should not delay thrombectomy workup. Idarucizumab reverses dabigatran, not apixaban.
reasoning: NIHSS 18 highly suggestive of large vessel occlusion
Thrombectomy indicated for LVO up to 24 hours in select patients
Thrombectomy does not require coagulation reversal
Apixaban (factor Xa inhibitor) not reversed by idarucizumab (dabigatran reversal)

evidence.title: AHA/ASA Acute Ischemic Stroke Guidelines 2019
evidence.cite: Powers WJ, et al. Stroke. 2019;50:e344-e418
points: Thrombectomy for LVO within 6 hours (Class I)
Extended window thrombectomy (6-24h) based on perfusion imaging
DOAC use: consider thrombectomy; tPA case-by-case based on anti-Xa level
Andexanet alfa reverses factor Xa inhibitors (not idarucizumab)

distractors.Administer IV alteplase immediately: Recent apixaban use (within 48 hours) is a relative contraindication to tPA. Anti-Xa level should be checked; if elevated, tPA is contraindicated.
distractors.Check anti-Xa level before any intervent: While anti-Xa level is useful, it should not delay CTA/thrombectomy evaluation. Thrombectomy can proceed regardless of anticoagulation status.
distractors.Administer idarucizumab followed by IV a: Idarucizumab reverses dabigatran (direct thrombin inhibitor), not apixaban (factor Xa inhibitor). This would be ineffective.
distractors.Start aspirin and admit to stroke unit: This severe stroke warrants aggressive reperfusion therapy evaluation, not medical management alone.

vignette:
A 68-year-old man with known HFrEF (EF 25%) presents with worsening dyspnea over 3 days. He has been adherent to his medications but admits to eating salty foods at a family gathering.

Past Medical History: HFrEF (ischemic), prior MI, HTN, DM2.

Medications: Sacubitril-valsartan, carvedilol, furosemide 40 mg daily, spironolactone, empagliflozin.

Vital Signs
BP 142/88 mm Hg
HR 92 bpm
SpO₂ 91% on RA

Physical Exam: JVP 14 cm, bibasilar crackles, S3 gallop, 2+ bilateral lower extremity edema.

Labs: BNP 1,840 pg/mL (baseline 400), Cr 1.4 (baseline 1.2), K 4.8.

CXR: Cardiomegaly with pulmonary vascular congestion and bilateral pleural effusions.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Increase oral furosemide to 80 mg twice daily
IV furosemide 40-80 mg bolus, then reassess
Hold sacubitril-valsartan due to rising creatinine

teaching.keyPoint: IV furosemide is the appropriate initial treatment for acute decompensated heart failure with volume overload. IV diuretics have more reliable absorption and faster onset than oral. Initial IV dose is typically 1-2.5x the home oral dose. This patient has clear volume overload (elevated JVP, edema, pulmonary congestion) with preserved blood pressure, making diuresis appropriate. Metolazone (thiazide) is added for diuretic resistance, not first-line. Dobutamine is for cardiogenic shock, not routine decompensation. Do not hold neurohormonal medications for mild creatinine rise.
reasoning: Acute decompensated HF with volume overload: IV diuretics first-line
IV furosemide more reliable than oral in acute setting
Dose: 1-2.5x home oral dose as IV
Monitor urine output, daily weights, electrolytes, renal function

evidence.title: AHA/ACC Heart Failure Guidelines 2022
evidence.cite: Heidenreich PA, et al. Circulation. 2022;145:e895-e1032
points: IV loop diuretics for acute decompensated HF (Class I)
Goal: adequate decongestion before discharge
Continue GDMT during hospitalization when possible
Ultrafiltration if diuretic resistance

distractors.Increase oral furosemide to 80 mg twice: Oral diuretics have variable absorption in acute HF due to gut edema. IV diuretics are preferred for reliable effect.
distractors.Add metolazone 5 mg daily: Metolazone is used for diuretic resistance (sequential nephron blockade) when loop diuretics alone are insufficient, not as initial therapy.
distractors.Start dobutamine infusion: Inotropes are reserved for cardiogenic shock with hypoperfusion. This patient has adequate blood pressure and does not need inotropic support.
distractors.Hold sacubitril-valsartan due to rising: Mild creatinine rise (0.2 mg/dL) is acceptable with neurohormonal therapy. Holding these agents is associated with worse outcomes. Continue unless severe decline.

vignette:
A 28-year-old woman develops diffuse urticaria, facial swelling, and difficulty breathing within 10 minutes of receiving IV penicillin for cellulitis. She has no known allergies.

Vital Signs
BP 82/50 mm Hg
HR 128 bpm
SpO₂ 88% on RA

Physical Exam: Diffuse urticaria and angioedema, stridor audible, using accessory muscles.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: IM epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg in the lateral thigh

teaching.keyPoint: IM epinephrine is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis and should be given immediately. The lateral thigh (vastus lateralis) provides rapid absorption. Dose is 0.3-0.5 mg (0.3-0.5 mL of 1:1000) IM for adults. IV epinephrine is reserved for refractory anaphylaxis or cardiac arrest and requires careful dosing/monitoring. Antihistamines and steroids are adjunctive but do NOT treat the life-threatening manifestations. Delay in epinephrine is associated with fatal anaphylaxis.
reasoning: Anaphylaxis with respiratory compromise + hypotension = severe
Epinephrine IM first-line - do not delay
IM preferred over subcutaneous (faster absorption)
Repeat every 5-15 minutes if needed

evidence.title: WAO Anaphylaxis Guidelines 2020
evidence.cite: Cardona V, et al. World Allergy Organ J. 2020;13:100472
points: Epinephrine IM is first-line (no absolute contraindications)
Lateral thigh preferred injection site
Antihistamines/steroids are adjunctive only
Observe 4-6 hours minimum for biphasic reaction

distractors.IV diphenhydramine 50 mg: Antihistamines are adjunctive therapy only. They do not reverse bronchospasm or hypotension and should never delay epinephrine.
distractors.IV methylprednisolone 125 mg: Corticosteroids may help prevent biphasic reactions but have no immediate effect on anaphylaxis. They should not delay epinephrine.
distractors.Nebulized albuterol: Albuterol may help bronchospasm but does not address the systemic vasodilation and cardiovascular collapse. Epinephrine treats all manifestations.
distractors.IV epinephrine 0.1 mg slow push: IV epinephrine is reserved for refractory anaphylaxis or cardiac arrest due to risk of arrhythmias. IM is first-line for most anaphylaxis cases.

vignette:
A 24-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. She ran out of insulin 2 days ago.

Vital Signs
BP 98/60 mm Hg
HR 118 bpm
RR 28/min (Kussmaul)

Labs: Glucose 520 mg/dL, Na 128 mEq/L, K 5.4 mEq/L, Cl 92 mEq/L, HCO3 8 mEq/L, AG 28, pH 7.12, serum ketones large.

Corrected sodium: Na + 1.6 × [(glucose - 100)/100] = 128 + 6.7 = 134.7 mEq/L
stem: After initial normal saline bolus, which of the following is the most appropriate fluid for ongoing resuscitation?

teaching.keyPoint: 0.45% NaCl (half-normal saline) is appropriate for ongoing fluid resuscitation in DKA when the corrected sodium is normal or elevated. Initial resuscitation uses 0.9% NS (1-1.5 L first hour), then fluid choice depends on corrected sodium: if corrected Na is low, continue NS; if corrected Na is normal or high, switch to 0.45% NaCl. This patient's corrected Na is ~135 (normal range). Once glucose reaches 200-250 mg/dL, add dextrose (D5 0.45% NaCl) to prevent hypoglycemia while continuing insulin to close the anion gap.
reasoning: DKA causes severe dehydration (average 6-9L deficit)
Initial: NS 1-1.5L in first hour
Subsequent: 0.45% NaCl if corrected Na normal/high
Add D5 when glucose <250 to prevent hypoglycemia

evidence.title: ADA DKA Management Standards
evidence.cite: Kitabchi AE, et al. Diabetes Care. 2009;32:1335-1343
points: Initial NS 15-20 mL/kg/hr first hour
0.45% NaCl if corrected Na normal or elevated
0.9% NaCl if corrected Na low
Add dextrose when glucose <250 mg/dL

distractors.Continue 0.9% NaCl at 250-500 mL/hr: With a corrected sodium in the normal range, continuing isotonic saline risks hypernatremia as glucose falls. Half-normal saline provides free water.
distractors.D5W at 250-500 mL/hr: D5W is not appropriate early in DKA management. Dextrose is added when glucose falls to 200-250 mg/dL, not initially.
distractors.Lactated Ringer at 250-500 mL/hr: LR contains lactate which is converted to bicarbonate, potentially causing alkalosis. It also contains potassium, complicating management. NS or half-NS preferred.
distractors.D5 0.45% NaCl at 250-500 mL/hr: Dextrose-containing fluids are used once glucose reaches 200-250 mg/dL, not initially when glucose is >500.

vignette:
A 58-year-old man with alcoholic cirrhosis presents with hematemesis. He vomited approximately 500 mL of bright red blood in the ED.

Past Medical History: Cirrhosis (Child-Pugh C), prior variceal bleeding 1 year ago, ascites.

Vital Signs
BP 86/52 mm Hg
HR 124 bpm

Labs: Hgb 7.2 g/dL, Platelets 58,000, INR 2.1, Cr 1.8.

Two large-bore IVs have been placed and fluid resuscitation initiated.
stem: In addition to blood transfusion, which combination of interventions is most appropriate?

options.text: Octreotide, ceftriaxone, and EGD within 12 hours
Vasopressin, vitamin K, and emergent TIPS
Octreotide, tranexamic acid, and Blakemore tube placement

teaching.keyPoint: Octreotide, prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone), and EGD within 12 hours is the appropriate management for suspected variceal bleeding. Vasoactive drugs (octreotide) reduce portal pressure. Prophylactic antibiotics significantly reduce mortality and rebleeding in cirrhotic GI bleed. PPI is NOT indicated for variceal bleeding (it's for peptic ulcer disease). Transfuse restrictively (target Hgb 7-8 g/dL). FFP does not improve outcomes in cirrhosis. EGD for diagnosis and banding/sclerotherapy within 12 hours.
reasoning: Variceal bleed management: vasoactive drug + antibiotics + EGD
Ceftriaxone reduces mortality (not just infection risk)
Restrictive transfusion (Hgb 7-8) reduces rebleeding
PPI is for peptic ulcer, not varices

evidence.title: AASLD Variceal Hemorrhage Practice Guidance
evidence.cite: Garcia-Tsao G, et al. Hepatology. 2017
points: Vasoactive drugs (octreotide/terlipressin) for up to 5 days
Ceftriaxone 1g IV daily for 7 days
Restrictive transfusion strategy
EGD within 12 hours

distractors.Octreotide, PPI, and emergent EGD: PPI is not indicated for variceal bleeding. Prophylactic antibiotics are critical and reduce mortality.
distractors.PPI, FFP, and platelet transfusion: FFP and platelet transfusion do not improve outcomes in cirrhotic bleeding. PPI is for ulcers, not varices.
distractors.Vasopressin, vitamin K, and emergent TIP: Vasopressin has more side effects than octreotide. TIPS is rescue therapy for failed endoscopic management, not first-line.
distractors.Octreotide, tranexamic acid, and Blakemo: Tranexamic acid is not standard for variceal bleeding. Balloon tamponade is rescue therapy only for uncontrolled bleeding, not initial management.

vignette:
A 19-year-old man presents with periumbilical pain that migrated to the right lower quadrant over 18 hours. He has anorexia and one episode of vomiting.

Vital Signs
Temp 100.4°F
HR 92 bpm

Physical Exam: RLQ tenderness with guarding, positive McBurney point tenderness, positive Rovsing sign, no rebound.

Labs: WBC 14,500 with left shift.

CT Abdomen: Dilated appendix 10 mm with periappendiceal fat stranding, no abscess, no perforation.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

options.text: IV antibiotics alone with outpatient follow-up

teaching.keyPoint: Appendectomy within 24 hours is standard of care for uncomplicated acute appendicitis. This patient has classic presentation with CT confirmation. Studies show outcomes are similar whether surgery is performed within 2 hours or within 24 hours for uncomplicated cases. Laparoscopic approach is preferred. Antibiotics-alone strategy has higher recurrence rates and is not first-line in most guidelines. Percutaneous drainage is for complicated appendicitis with abscess. Observation is inappropriate with confirmed diagnosis.
reasoning: Uncomplicated appendicitis: appendectomy within 24 hours
Laparoscopic approach preferred
Emergent surgery (<2 hours) not required for uncomplicated cases
Antibiotics alone: higher recurrence, reserved for select cases

evidence.title: SAGES Appendicitis Guidelines
evidence.cite: Korndorffer JR, et al. Surg Endosc. 2021
points: Laparoscopic appendectomy is gold standard
Surgery within 24 hours for uncomplicated appendicitis
Perioperative antibiotics reduce infection
Antibiotics-first may be considered in select patients

distractors.IV antibiotics alone with outpatient fol: While antibiotics-first is being studied, it has higher recurrence/failure rates. Surgery remains standard of care per most guidelines.
distractors.Emergent appendectomy within 2 hours: Outcomes are similar whether performed within 2 hours or 24 hours for uncomplicated appendicitis. Emergent surgery reserved for perforation/peritonitis.
distractors.Percutaneous drainage and interval appen: This approach is for complicated appendicitis with abscess, not uncomplicated disease.
distractors.Observation with serial abdominal exams: Observation delays treatment and risks progression. With confirmed appendicitis, surgery is indicated.

vignette:
A 45-year-old woman presents with acute dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain for 6 hours. She had a long flight from Europe 4 days ago.

Vital Signs
BP 128/78 mm Hg
HR 102 bpm
SpO₂ 94% on RA

CTPA: Segmental PE in right lower lobe. No RV dilation.

Labs: Troponin <0.01, BNP 45 pg/mL, Cr 0.9.

Echocardiogram: Normal RV size and function.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment?

options.text: Rivaroxaban 15 mg twice daily for 21 days, then 20 mg daily
Warfarin with enoxaparin bridge, INR goal 2-3

teaching.keyPoint: DOAC (rivaroxaban) is first-line anticoagulation for acute PE in patients without contraindications. This patient has low-risk PE: hemodynamically stable, normal RV function, normal biomarkers. DOACs are preferred over warfarin for most patients (no bridging, no monitoring, comparable efficacy). Duration for provoked PE is 3 months. Thrombolysis is reserved for high-risk (massive) PE with hemodynamic instability. IVC filter only if anticoagulation absolutely contraindicated.
reasoning: Low-risk PE: anticoagulation alone, no thrombolysis
DOACs preferred over VKA for most patients
Provoked PE (travel): 3 months treatment
Assess bleeding risk before starting anticoagulation

evidence.title: ESC PE Guidelines 2019
evidence.cite: Konstantinides SV, et al. Eur Heart J. 2020
points: DOACs preferred over VKA for PE
Risk stratification guides management intensity
Thrombolysis for high-risk (hemodynamically unstable) PE
Duration: provoked 3 months, unprovoked extended

distractors.Thrombolytic therapy with alteplase: Thrombolysis is reserved for high-risk PE with hemodynamic instability. This patient is low-risk with stable vitals and normal RV function.
distractors.Warfarin with enoxaparin bridge, INR goa: While effective, DOACs are now preferred over warfarin for most PE patients due to convenience and safety profile.
distractors.IVC filter placement: IVC filters are indicated only when anticoagulation is absolutely contraindicated. This patient has no contraindication to anticoagulation.
distractors.Catheter-directed thrombolysis: CDT is considered for intermediate-high risk PE, not low-risk PE with normal RV function and biomarkers.

vignette:
A 68-year-old man with diabetes and hypertension is hospitalized for pneumonia. On day 3, his creatinine has risen from 1.2 to 2.8 mg/dL. Urine output is 20 mL/hr.

Medications: Vancomycin, piperacillin-tazobactam, lisinopril (held), metformin (held), IV NS.

Current Status
BP 108/68 mm Hg
HR 88 bpm

Physical Exam: Flat JVP, dry mucous membranes, no edema.

Urinalysis: Bland sediment, no casts, trace protein.

Urine Studies: FeNa 0.4%, Urine Na 12 mEq/L.
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Start furosemide to improve urine output
IV fluid bolus and reassess volume status

teaching.keyPoint: IV fluid bolus is appropriate for prerenal AKI. This patient has clinical signs of hypovolemia (flat JVP, dry mucous membranes) and urine studies consistent with prerenal azotemia (FeNa <1%, low urine Na). The kidney is appropriately retaining sodium in response to decreased perfusion. Treatment is volume repletion. Diuretics would worsen prerenal AKI. Dialysis is not indicated without uremic symptoms, refractory hyperkalemia, acidosis, or volume overload. The bland sediment argues against intrinsic renal disease.
reasoning: FeNa <1% indicates prerenal etiology
Clinical hypovolemia + low urine sodium = volume depletion
Treatment: correct underlying cause (volume)
Avoid nephrotoxins, adjust medication dosing

evidence.title: KDIGO AKI Guidelines
evidence.cite: Kidney Int Suppl. 2012
points: FeNa <1% suggests prerenal AKI
Volume resuscitation for hypovolemic AKI
Avoid diuretics in prerenal AKI
Dialysis for refractory complications

distractors.Start furosemide to improve urine output: Diuretics worsen prerenal AKI by further depleting volume. They may be indicated for volume overload but are contraindicated here.
distractors.Emergent hemodialysis: Dialysis indications: refractory hyperkalemia, acidosis, uremic symptoms, volume overload. This patient has none of these.
distractors.Renal biopsy: Biopsy is for unexplained intrinsic renal disease. This patient has clear prerenal etiology based on urine studies and clinical assessment.
distractors.Continue current management and monitor: The patient is hypovolemic and requires active fluid resuscitation, not just observation.
